revision.text: #redirect[[en:Tatsuyama_quarry]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]

'''竜山石採石遺跡'''（たつやまいし さいくついせきあと）は[[兵庫県]][[高砂市]]の[[竜山]]山中にある[[竜山石]]の採掘[[遺跡]]。[[平成26年]][[10月6日]]に国の史跡に指定<ref>[http://www.hyogo-c.ed.jp/~board-bo/kisya26/2606/2606195.pdf 史跡名勝天然記念物の指定について（国指定史跡）]</ref><ref>[http://www.sankei.com/region/news/140621/rgn1406210026-n1.html 石の宝殿及び竜山石採石遺跡の国史跡指定、文科相に答申 兵庫]</ref>。
==概要== 竜山石は[[凝灰岩]]で形成されていて加工に適した強度と粘りを有している。[[古墳時代]]から現代に至るまで採掘され、特に西日本の[[古墳]]や[[石仏]]・[[石垣]]などに使われている。近年でも[[姫路城]]の石垣や鳥取藩主池田家墓所の[[玉垣]]、[[山本能楽堂]]など文化財の修復石材に使われている<ref>[http://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/shakai/201501/0007703615.shtml 文化財修復で脚光 １７００年の歴史持つ高砂・竜山石]</ref>。
==史跡== 全体で162箇所（約67万[[平方メートル]]）の採石遺構のうち31箇所（約11万平方メートル）が史跡に指定されている。古代以前の採石遺跡が指定されるのは全国初。
==関連項目== *[[竜山石]] *[[石の宝殿]]
==外部リンク== *[http://www.city.takasago.hyogo.jp/index.cfm/7,36295,111,html 国史跡「石の宝殿及び竜山石採石遺跡」] *[https://kotobank.jp/word/竜山石-321057 竜山石]
{{Pref-stub|pref=兵庫県}} {{Mountain-stub}} {{Architecture-stub}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:たつやまいしさいくついせき}}
[[Category:高砂市]] [[Category:兵庫県にある国指定の史跡]]

revision.comment: 史跡名の正確化、見出し整理（定義のシェイプ）、[[WP:JPE]]に基づき全角空白を半角化
revision.text:
'''竜山石採石遺跡'''（たつやまいし さいせきいせき）は、[[兵庫県]][[高砂市]]にある[[遺跡]]。
== 概要 == [[古墳時代]]から現代に至るまで、市内で産出されている石材・[[竜山石]]（たつやまいし）を採石した形跡がみられる、総面積67万[[平方メートル]]、162か所におよぶ遺構<ref name="shitei">[http://www.hyogo-c.ed.jp/~board-bo/kisya26/2606/2606195.pdf 史跡名勝天然記念物の指定について（国指定史跡）] 兵庫県教育委員会</ref>である。このうち、[[竜山 (兵庫県)|竜山]]（たつやま）と宝殿山（ほうでんやま）にまたがる約11万平方メートル、31か所については、[[2014年]]（[[平成]]26年）[[10月6日]]に、「'''[[石の宝殿]]及び竜山石採石遺跡'''（いしのほうでん および たつやまいしさいせきいせき）」として、他の史跡群とともに国の[[史跡]]に一括指定された。古代以前の採石遺跡としては、初の国指定史跡である<ref name="shitei" /><ref>[http://www.sankei.com/region/news/140621/rgn1406210026-n1.html 石の宝殿及び竜山石採石遺跡の国史跡指定、文科相に答申 兵庫] [[産経新聞]]</ref>。 === 竜山石 === 竜山石は、加工に適した強度と粘りを有する[[凝灰岩]]である。採石場の近くを流れる[[法華山谷川]]（洗川）からの舟運で西日本各地に運ばれ、時代を問わず建築物や[[石仏]]等に利用された。古墳時代中後期の[[長持形石棺]]・[[家形石棺]]、[[奈良時代]]の[[平城宮]]および[[恭仁京|恭仁宮]]の[[礎石]]、近代の[[旧造幣寮鋳造所正面玄関|旧造幣寮貨幣鋳造所]]、[[住友ビルディング]]、[[名古屋市公会堂]]の外壁が知られる<ref name="shitei" />。
文化財修復用の石材としても利用される。[[姫路城]]の[[石垣]]、鳥取藩主[[池田氏|池田家]]墓所の[[玉垣]]、[[山本能楽堂]]などが知られる<ref>[http://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/shakai/201501/0007703615.shtml 文化財修復で脚光 1700年の歴史持つ高砂・竜山石] [[神戸新聞]]</ref>。
==脚注== {{脚注ヘルプ}} <references />
==関連項目== *[[竜山石]] *[[石の宝殿]]
==外部リンク== * [http://www.city.takasago.hyogo.jp/index.cfm/7,36295,111,html 国史跡「石の宝殿及び竜山石採石遺跡」] 高砂市 * [https://kotobank.jp/word/竜山石-321057 竜山石] コトバンク
{{coord|34|46|51.0|N|134|47|41.0|E|region:JP-28|display=title}} {{Mountain-stub}} {{Architecture-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:たつやまいしさいせきいせき}} [[Category:高砂市]] [[Category:高砂市の歴史]] [[Category:兵庫県にある国指定の史跡]]

'''竜山石採石遺跡'''（たつやまいし さいせきいせき）は、[[兵庫県]][[高砂市]]にある[[遺跡]]。
== 概要 == [[古墳時代]]から現代に至るまで、市内で産出されている石材・[[竜山石]]（たつやまいし）を採石した形跡がみられる、総面積67万[[平方メートル]]、162か所におよぶ遺構<ref name="shitei">[http://www.hyogo-c.ed.jp/~board-bo/kisya26/2606/2606195.pdf 史跡名勝天然記念物の指定について（国指定史跡）] 兵庫県教育委員会</ref>である。このうち、[[竜山 (兵庫県)|竜山]]（たつやま）と宝殿山（ほうでんやま）にまたがる約11万平方メートル、31か所については、[[2014年]]（[[平成]]26年）[[10月6日]]に、「'''[[石の宝殿]]及び竜山石採石遺跡'''（いしのほうでん および たつやまいしさいせきいせき）」として、他の史跡群とともに国の[[史跡]]に一括指定された。古代以前の採石遺跡としては、初の国指定史跡である<ref name="shitei" /><ref>[http://www.sankei.com/region/news/140621/rgn1406210026-n1.html 石の宝殿及び竜山石採石遺跡の国史跡指定、文科相に答申 兵庫] [[産経新聞]]</ref>。 === 竜山石 === 竜山石は、加工に適した強度と粘りを有する[[凝灰岩]]である。採石場の近くを流れる[[法華山谷川]]（洗川）からの舟運で西日本各地に運ばれ、時代を問わず建築物や[[石仏]]等に利用された。古墳時代中後期の[[長持形石棺]]・[[家形石棺]]、[[奈良時代]]の[[平城宮]]および[[恭仁京|恭仁宮]]の[[礎石]]、近代の[[旧造幣寮鋳造所正面玄関|旧造幣寮貨幣鋳造所]]、[[住友ビルディング]]、[[名古屋市公会堂]]の外壁が知られる<ref name="shitei" />。
文化財修復用の石材としても利用される。[[姫路城]]の[[石垣]]、鳥取藩主[[池田氏|池田家]]墓所の[[玉垣]]、[[山本能楽堂]]などが知られる<ref>[http://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/shakai/201501/0007703615.shtml 文化財修復で脚光 1700年の歴史持つ高砂・竜山石] [[神戸新聞]]</ref>。
==脚注== {{脚注ヘルプ}} <references />
==関連項目== *[[竜山石]] *[[石の宝殿]]
==外部リンク== * [http://www.city.takasago.hyogo.jp/index.cfm/7,36295,111,html 国史跡「石の宝殿及び竜山石採石遺跡」] 高砂市 * [https://kotobank.jp/word/竜山石-321057 竜山石] コトバンク

{{画像提供依頼|date=2017年2月10日|cat=高砂市|風景}} '''竜山石採石遺跡'''（たつやまいし さいせきいせき）は、[[兵庫県]][[高砂市]]にある[[遺跡]]。国の[[史跡]]に指定されている<ref name="文化遺産オンライン" />。
== 概要 == [[古墳時代]]から現代に至るまで、市内で産出されている石材・[[竜山石]]（たつやまいし）を採石した形跡がみられる、総面積67万[[平方メートル]]、162か所におよぶ遺構<ref name="shitei">[http://www.hyogo-c.ed.jp/~board-bo/kisya26/2606/2606195.pdf 史跡名勝天然記念物の指定について（国指定史跡）] 兵庫県教育委員会</ref>である。このうち、[[竜山 (兵庫県)|竜山]]（たつやま）と宝殿山（ほうでんやま）にまたがる約11万平方メートル、31か所については、[[2014年]]（[[平成]]26年）[[10月6日]]に、「'''[[石の宝殿]]及び竜山石採石遺跡'''（いしのほうでん および たつやまいしさいせきいせき）」として、他の史跡群とともに国の[[史跡]]に一括指定された<ref name="文化遺産オンライン">{{文化遺産オンライン|210377|石の宝殿及び竜山石採石遺跡}}</ref>。古代以前の採石遺跡としては、初の国指定の史跡である<ref name="shitei" /><ref>[http://www.sankei.com/region/news/140621/rgn1406210026-n1.html 石の宝殿及び竜山石採石遺跡の国史跡指定、文科相に答申 兵庫] [[産経新聞]]</ref>。 === 竜山石 === 竜山石は、加工に適した強度と粘りを有する[[凝灰岩]]である。採石場の近くを流れる[[法華山谷川]]（洗川）からの舟運で西日本各地に運ばれ、時代を問わず建築物や[[石仏]]等に利用された。古墳時代中後期の[[長持形石棺]]・[[家形石棺]]、[[奈良時代]]の[[平城宮]]および[[恭仁京|恭仁宮]]の[[礎石]]、近代の[[旧造幣寮鋳造所正面玄関|旧造幣寮貨幣鋳造所]]、[[住友ビルディング]]、[[名古屋市公会堂]]の外壁が知られる<ref name="shitei" />。
文化財修復用の石材としても利用される。[[姫路城]]の[[石垣]]、鳥取藩主[[池田氏|池田家]]墓所の[[玉垣]]、[[山本能楽堂]]などが知られる<ref>[http://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/shakai/201501/0007703615.shtml 文化財修復で脚光 1700年の歴史持つ高砂・竜山石] [[神戸新聞]]</ref>。

文化財修復用の石材としても利用される。[[姫路城]]の[[石垣]]、鳥取藩主[[池田氏|池田家]]墓所の[[玉垣]]、[[山本能楽堂]]などが知られる<ref>[http://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/shakai/201501/0007703615.shtml 文化財修復で脚光 1700年の歴史持つ高砂・竜山石] [[神戸新聞]]</ref>。

{{画像提供依頼|date=2017年2月10日|cat=高砂市|風景}} '''竜山石採石遺跡'''（たつやまいし さいせきいせき）は、[[兵庫県]][[高砂市]]にある[[遺跡]]。国の[[史跡]]に指定されている<ref name="文化遺産オンライン" />。
==概要== [[古墳時代]]から現代に至るまでの約1700年間、市内で産出されている石材・[[竜山石採石遺跡#竜山石|竜山石]]（たつやまいし）を採石した形跡がみられる、総面積67万[[平方メートル]]、162か所におよぶ遺構<ref name="shitei">[http://www.hyogo-c.ed.jp/~board-bo/kisya26/2606/2606195.pdf 史跡名勝天然記念物の指定について（国指定史跡）] 兵庫県教育委員会 {{リンク切れ|date=2020年7月}}</ref>である。このうち、[[竜山 (兵庫県)|竜山]]（たつやま）と宝殿山（ほうでんやま）にまたがる約11万平方メートル、31か所については、[[2014年]]（[[平成]]26年）[[10月6日]]に、「'''[[石の宝殿]]及び竜山石採石遺跡'''（いしのほうでん および たつやまいしさいせきいせき）」として、他の史跡群とともに国の[[史跡]]に一括指定された<ref name="文化遺産オンライン">{{文化遺産オンライン|210377|石の宝殿及び竜山石採石遺跡}}</ref>。古代以前の採石遺跡としては、初の国指定の史跡である<ref name="shitei" /><ref>[http://www.sankei.com/region/news/140621/rgn1406210026-n1.html 石の宝殿及び竜山石採石遺跡の国史跡指定、文科相に答申 兵庫] [[産経新聞]]</ref>。 ===竜山石=== 竜山石は、[[加古川]]下流右岸に産する[[流紋岩]]質[[溶結凝灰岩]]の[[石材]]上の呼称。約1億年前の[[白亜紀]]後期の[[火山活動]]によって噴出した[[火砕流]]が厚く堆積したものであり、石材としては、加工に適した強度と粘りを有する。
採石場の近くを流れる[[法華山谷川]]（洗川）からの舟運で西日本各地に運ばれ、時代を問わず建築物や[[石仏]]等に利用された。古墳時代中後期の[[大仙陵古墳|大仙陵古墳（仁徳天皇陵）]][[石棺]]、[[長持形石棺]]・[[家形石棺]]、[[奈良時代]]の[[平城宮]]および[[恭仁京|恭仁宮]]の[[礎石]]、近現代では[[皇居]][[吹上御苑]][[御所]]、[[国会議事堂]]のほか、[[旧造幣寮鋳造所正面玄関|旧造幣寮貨幣鋳造所]]、[[住友ビルディング|旧住友銀行大阪本店ビル（現・住友ビルディング）]]、[[名古屋市公会堂]]の外壁が知られる<ref name="shitei" />。
文化財修復用の石材としても利用される。[[姫路城]]の[[石垣]]、鳥取藩主[[池田氏|池田家]]墓所の[[玉垣]]、[[山本能楽堂]]などが知られる<ref>[http://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/shakai/201501/0007703615.shtml 文化財修復で脚光 1700年の歴史持つ高砂・竜山石] [[神戸新聞]]{{リンク切れ|date=2020年7月}}</ref>。

[[File:Tatsuyama stone.jpg|thumb|石の宝殿及び竜山石採石遺跡]] '''竜山石採石遺跡'''（たつやまいし さいせきいせき）は、[[兵庫県]][[高砂市]]にある[[遺跡]]。国の[[史跡]]に指定されている<ref name="文化遺産オンライン" />。
==概要== [[古墳時代]]から現代に至るまでの約1700年間、市内で産出されている石材・[[竜山石採石遺跡#竜山石|竜山石]]（たつやまいし）を採石した形跡がみられる、総面積67万[[平方メートル]]、162か所におよぶ遺構<ref name="shitei">[http://www.hyogo-c.ed.jp/~board-bo/kisya26/2606/2606195.pdf 史跡名勝天然記念物の指定について（国指定史跡）] 兵庫県教育委員会 {{リンク切れ|date=2020年7月}}</ref>である。このうち、[[竜山 (兵庫県)|竜山]]（たつやま）と宝殿山（ほうでんやま）にまたがる約11万平方メートル、31か所については、[[2014年]]（[[平成]]26年）[[10月6日]]に、「'''[[石の宝殿]]及び竜山石採石遺跡'''（いしのほうでん および たつやまいしさいせきいせき）」として、他の史跡群とともに国の[[史跡]]に一括指定された<ref name="文化遺産オンライン">{{文化遺産オンライン|210377|石の宝殿及び竜山石採石遺跡}}</ref>。古代以前の採石遺跡としては、初の国指定の史跡である<ref name="shitei" /><ref>[http://www.sankei.com/region/news/140621/rgn1406210026-n1.html 石の宝殿及び竜山石採石遺跡の国史跡指定、文科相に答申 兵庫] [[産経新聞]]</ref>。 ===竜山石=== 竜山石は、[[加古川]]下流右岸に産する[[流紋岩]]質[[溶結凝灰岩]]の[[石材]]上の呼称。約1億年前の[[白亜紀]]後期の[[火山活動]]によって噴出した[[火砕流]]が厚く堆積したものであり、石材としては、加工に適した強度と粘りを有する。
採石場の近くを流れる[[法華山谷川]]（洗川）からの舟運で西日本各地に運ばれ、時代を問わず建築物や[[石仏]]等に利用された。古墳時代中後期の[[大仙陵古墳|大仙陵古墳（仁徳天皇陵）]][[石棺]]、[[長持形石棺]]・[[家形石棺]]、[[奈良時代]]の[[平城宮]]および[[恭仁京|恭仁宮]]の[[礎石]]、近現代では[[皇居]][[吹上御苑]][[御所]]、[[国会議事堂]]のほか、[[旧造幣寮鋳造所正面玄関|旧造幣寮貨幣鋳造所]]、[[住友ビルディング|旧住友銀行大阪本店ビル（現・住友ビルディング）]]、[[名古屋市公会堂]]の外壁が知られる<ref name="shitei" />。
文化財修復用の石材としても利用される。[[姫路城]]の[[石垣]]、鳥取藩主[[池田氏|池田家]]墓所の[[玉垣]]、[[山本能楽堂]]などが知られる<ref>[http://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/shakai/201501/0007703615.shtml 文化財修復で脚光 1700年の歴史持つ高砂・竜山石] [[神戸新聞]]{{リンク切れ|date=2020年7月}}</ref>。
==脚注== {{脚注ヘルプ}} <references />
==関連項目== *[[竜山石]] *[[石の宝殿]]
==外部リンク== *[http://www.city.takasago.lg.jp/index.cfm/18,36295,175,html 国史跡「石の宝殿及び竜山石採石遺跡」] 高砂市 *[https://kotobank.jp/word/竜山石-321057 竜山石] コトバンク
{{Coord|34|46|51.0|N|134|47|41.0|E|region:JP-28|display=title}} {{Mountain-stub}} {{Architecture-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:たつやまいしさいせきいせき}} [[Category:高砂市]] [[Category:高砂市の歴史]] [[Category:兵庫県にある国指定の史跡]] [[Category:採石場]]

[[File:Tatsuyama stone.jpg|thumb|石の宝殿及び竜山石採石遺跡]] '''竜山石採石遺跡'''（たつやまいし さいせきいせき）は、[[兵庫県]][[高砂市]]内の[[遺跡]]である。国の[[史跡]]に指定されている<ref name="文化遺産オンライン" />。
== 概要 == [[古墳時代]]から現代に至るまでの約1700年間、高砂市内で産出されている石材・[[竜山石採石遺跡#竜山石|竜山石]]（たつやまいし）を採石した形跡が見られる、総面積67万[[平方メートル]]、162か所におよぶ遺構である<ref name="shitei">[http://www.hyogo-c.ed.jp/~board-bo/kisya26/2606/2606195.pdf 史跡名勝天然記念物の指定について（国指定史跡）] 兵庫県教育委員会 {{リンク切れ|date=2020年7月}}</ref>。このうち、[[竜山 (兵庫県)|竜山]]（たつやま）と宝殿山（ほうでんやま）にまたがる約11万平方メートル、31か所については、[[2014年]][[10月6日]]に、「'''[[石の宝殿]]及び竜山石採石遺跡'''（いしのほうでん および たつやまいしさいせきいせき）」として、他の史跡群と一緒に、国の[[史跡]]に一括指定された<ref name="文化遺産オンライン">{{文化遺産オンライン|210377|石の宝殿及び竜山石採石遺跡}}</ref>。古代以前の採石遺跡としては、初の国指定の史跡である<ref name="shitei" /><ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20140621-DL7M3INHVFINPOAXAFCBCSR2PE/ 石の宝殿及び竜山石採石遺跡の国史跡指定、文科相に答申 兵庫] [[産経新聞]]</ref>。
=== 竜山石 === 竜山石は、[[加古川]]下流右岸に産する[[流紋岩]]質[[溶結凝灰岩]]の[[石材]]上の呼称。約1億年前の[[白亜紀]]後期の[[火山活動]]によって噴出した[[火砕流]]が厚く堆積して形成された。石材としては、加工に適した強度と粘りを有する。
採石場の近くを流れる[[法華山谷川]]（洗川）からの舟運で西日本各地に運ばれ、時代を問わず建築物や[[石仏]]等に利用された。古墳時代中後期の[[大仙陵古墳|大仙陵古墳（仁徳天皇陵）]][[石棺]]、[[長持形石棺]]・[[家形石棺]]、[[奈良時代]]の[[平城宮]]および[[恭仁京|恭仁宮]]の[[礎石]]、近現代では[[皇居]][[吹上御苑]][[御所]]、[[国会議事堂]]のほか、[[旧造幣寮鋳造所正面玄関|旧造幣寮貨幣鋳造所]]、[[住友ビルディング|旧住友銀行大阪本店ビル（現・住友ビルディング）]]、[[名古屋市公会堂]]の外壁が知られる<ref name="shitei" />。また道標の石材として使用された事例も知られる<ref>井原卓也『東播磨の道標をたずねて』神戸新聞総合出版センター（2004年4月）ISBN4-343-00267-5、146ページ</ref>。
文化財修復用の石材としても利用される。[[姫路城]]の[[石垣]]、鳥取藩主[[池田氏|池田家]]墓所の[[玉垣]]、[[山本能楽堂]]などが知られる<ref>[http://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/shakai/201501/0007703615.shtml 文化財修復で脚光 1700年の歴史持つ高砂・竜山石] [[神戸新聞]]{{リンク切れ|date=2020年7月}}</ref>。

== 概要 == [[古墳時代]]から現代に至るまでの約1700年間、高砂市内で産出されている石材・[[竜山石採石遺跡#竜山石|竜山石]]（たつやまいし）を採石した形跡が見られる、総面積67万[[平方メートル]]、162か所におよぶ遺構である<ref name="shitei">[http://www.hyogo-c.ed.jp/~board-bo/kisya26/2606/2606195.pdf 史跡名勝天然記念物の指定について（国指定史跡）] 兵庫県教育委員会 {{リンク切れ|date=2020年7月}}</ref>。このうち、[[竜山 (兵庫県)|竜山]]（たつやま）と宝殿山（ほうでんやま）にまたがる約11万平方メートル、31か所については、[[2014年]][[10月6日]]に、「'''[[石の宝殿]]及び竜山石採石遺跡'''（いしのほうでん および たつやまいしさいせきいせき）」として、他の史跡群と一緒に、国の[[史跡]]に一括指定された<ref name="文化遺産オンライン">{{文化遺産オンライン|210377|石の宝殿及び竜山石採石遺跡}}</ref>。古代以前の採石遺跡としては、初の国指定の史跡である<ref name="shitei" /><ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20140621-DL7M3INHVFINPOAXAFCBCSR2PE/ 石の宝殿及び竜山石採石遺跡の国史跡指定、文科相に答申 兵庫] [[産経新聞]]</ref>。
=== 竜山石 === 竜山石は、[[加古川]]下流右岸に産する[[流紋岩]]質[[溶結凝灰岩]]の[[石材]]上の呼称。約1億年前の[[白亜紀]]後期の[[火山活動]]によって噴出した[[火砕流]]が厚く堆積して形成された。石材としては、加工に適した強度と粘りを有する。
採石場の近くを流れる[[法華山谷川]]（洗川）からの舟運で西日本各地に運ばれ、時代を問わず建築物や[[石仏]]等に利用された。古墳時代中後期の[[大仙陵古墳|大仙陵古墳（仁徳天皇陵）]][[石棺]]、[[長持形石棺]]・[[家形石棺]]、[[奈良時代]]の[[平城宮]]および[[恭仁京|恭仁宮]]の[[礎石]]、近現代では[[皇居]][[吹上御苑]][[御所]]、[[国会議事堂]]のほか、[[旧造幣寮鋳造所正面玄関|旧造幣寮貨幣鋳造所]]、[[三井住友銀行大阪本店ビル]]（旧 住友ビルディング）、[[名古屋市公会堂]]の外壁が知られる<ref name="shitei" />。また道標の石材として使用された事例も知られる<ref>井原卓也『東播磨の道標をたずねて』神戸新聞総合出版センター（2004年4月）ISBN4-343-00267-5、146ページ</ref>。
文化財修復用の石材としても利用される。[[姫路城]]の[[石垣]]、鳥取藩主[[池田氏|池田家]]墓所の[[玉垣]]、[[山本能楽堂]]などが知られる<ref>[http://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/shakai/201501/0007703615.shtml 文化財修復で脚光 1700年の歴史持つ高砂・竜山石] [[神戸新聞]]{{リンク切れ|date=2020年7月}}</ref>。

採石場の近くを流れる[[法華山谷川]]（洗川）からの舟運で西日本各地に運ばれ、時代を問わず建築物や[[石仏]]等に利用された。古墳時代中後期の[[大仙陵古墳|大仙陵古墳（仁徳天皇陵）]][[石棺]]、[[長持形石棺]]・[[家形石棺]]、[[奈良時代]]の[[平城宮]]および[[恭仁京|恭仁宮]]の[[礎石]]、近現代では[[皇居]][[吹上御苑]][[御所]]、[[国会議事堂]]のほか、[[旧造幣寮鋳造所正面玄関|旧造幣寮貨幣鋳造所]]、[[三井住友銀行大阪本店ビル]]（旧 住友ビルディング）、[[名古屋市公会堂]]の外壁が知られる<ref name="shitei" />。また道標の石材として使用された事例も知られる<ref>井原卓也『東播磨の道標をたずねて』神戸新聞総合出版センター（2004年4月）ISBN 4-343-00267-5、146ページ</ref>。

[[File:Tatsuyama stone.jpg|thumb|石の宝殿及び竜山石採石遺跡]] '''竜山石採石遺跡'''（たつやまいし さいせきいせき）は、[[兵庫県]][[高砂市]]に位置する[[遺跡]]である。国の[[史跡]]に指定されている<ref name="文化遺産オンライン" />。
== 概要 == [[古墳時代]]から現代に至るまでの約1700年間、高砂市内で産出されている石材・[[竜山石採石遺跡#竜山石|竜山石]]（たつやまいし）を採石した形跡が見られる、総面積67万[[平方メートル]]、162か所におよぶ遺構である<ref name="shitei">[http://www.hyogo-c.ed.jp/~board-bo/kisya26/2606/2606195.pdf 史跡名勝天然記念物の指定について（国指定史跡）] 兵庫県教育委員会 {{リンク切れ|date=2020年7月}}</ref>。このうち、[[竜山 (兵庫県)|竜山]]（たつやま）と宝殿山（ほうでんやま）にまたがる約11万平方メートル、31か所については、[[2014年]][[10月6日]]に、「'''[[石の宝殿]]及び竜山石採石遺跡'''（いしのほうでん および たつやまいしさいせきいせき）」として、他の史跡群と一緒に、国の[[史跡]]に一括指定された<ref name="文化遺産オンライン">{{文化遺産オンライン|210377|石の宝殿及び竜山石採石遺跡}}</ref>。古代以前の採石遺跡としては、初の国指定の史跡である<ref name="shitei" /><ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20140621-DL7M3INHVFINPOAXAFCBCSR2PE/ 石の宝殿及び竜山石採石遺跡の国史跡指定、文科相に答申 兵庫] [[産経新聞]]</ref>。
=== 竜山石 === 竜山石は、[[加古川]]下流右岸の宝殿層から産する石材。従来は[[流紋岩]]質[[溶結凝灰岩]]とされていたが近年の調査研究により、約1億年前の[[白亜紀]]後期の[[火山活動]]により[[カルデラ]]湖底に噴出した[[マグマ]]が湖水によって急速に冷却、破砕されてできた流紋岩質の[[水中自破砕溶岩]]（[[ハイアロクラスタイト]]）であると特定されている<ref>[https://www.gsj.jp/data/50KGM/PDF/GSJ_MAP_G050_12071_2003_D.pdf 高砂地域の地質 第12章 資源地質 2.採石及び砕石] [[地質調査総合センター]]</ref>。石材としては、加工に適した強度と粘りを有する。色調は本来は淡緑色であるが酸化による色調変化があり、石材としては青、黃、赤系統の色がある。宝殿石とも呼ばれる。
採石場の近くを流れる[[法華山谷川]]（洗川）からの舟運で西日本各地に運ばれ、時代を問わず建築物や[[石仏]]等に利用された。古墳時代中後期の[[大仙陵古墳|大仙陵古墳（仁徳天皇陵）]][[石棺]]、[[長持形石棺]]・[[家形石棺]]、[[奈良時代]]の[[平城宮]]および[[恭仁京|恭仁宮]]の[[礎石]]、[[飛鳥大仏]]の台座、近現代では[[皇居]][[吹上御苑]][[御所]]、[[国会議事堂]]のほか、[[旧造幣寮鋳造所正面玄関|旧造幣寮貨幣鋳造所]]、[[三井住友銀行大阪本店ビル]]（旧 住友ビルディング）、[[名古屋市公会堂]]の外壁が知られる<ref name="shitei" />。また道標の石材として使用された事例も知られる<ref>井原卓也『東播磨の道標をたずねて』神戸新聞総合出版センター（2004年4月）ISBN 4-343-00267-5、146ページ</ref>。
文化財修復用の石材としても利用される。[[姫路城]]の[[石垣]]、鳥取藩主[[池田氏|池田家]]墓所の[[玉垣]]、[[山本能楽堂]]などが知られる<ref>[http://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/shakai/201501/0007703615.shtml 文化財修復で脚光 1700年の歴史持つ高砂・竜山石] [[神戸新聞]]{{リンク切れ|date=2020年7月}}</ref>。

[[File:Tatsuyama stone.jpg|thumb|石の宝殿及び竜山石採石遺跡]] '''竜山石採石遺跡'''（たつやまいし さいせきいせき）は、[[兵庫県]][[高砂市]]に位置する[[遺跡]]である。約200m離れた[[石の宝殿#生石神社の石宝殿|石の宝殿]]とともに国の[[史跡]]に指定されている<ref name="文化遺産オンライン" />。
== 概要 == [[古墳時代]]から現代に至るまでの約1700年間、高砂市内で産出されている石材・[[竜山石採石遺跡#竜山石|竜山石]]（たつやまいし）を採石した形跡が見られる、総面積67万[[平方メートル]]、162か所におよぶ遺構である<ref name="shitei">[http://www.hyogo-c.ed.jp/~board-bo/kisya26/2606/2606195.pdf 史跡名勝天然記念物の指定について（国指定史跡）] 兵庫県教育委員会 {{リンク切れ|date=2020年7月}}</ref>。このうち、[[竜山 (兵庫県)|竜山]]（たつやま）と宝殿山（ほうでんやま）にまたがる約11万平方メートル、31か所については、[[2014年]][[10月6日]]に、「'''石の宝殿及び竜山石採石遺跡'''（いしのほうでん および たつやまいしさいせきいせき）」として、他の史跡群と一緒に、国の[[史跡]]に一括指定された<ref name="文化遺産オンライン">{{文化遺産オンライン|210377|石の宝殿及び竜山石採石遺跡}}</ref>。古代以前の採石遺跡としては、初の国指定の史跡である<ref name="shitei" /><ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20140621-DL7M3INHVFINPOAXAFCBCSR2PE/ 石の宝殿及び竜山石採石遺跡の国史跡指定、文科相に答申 兵庫] [[産経新聞]]</ref>。
=== 竜山石 === 竜山石は、[[加古川]]下流右岸の宝殿層から産する石材。従来は[[凝灰岩]]の一種である[[流紋岩]]質[[溶結凝灰岩]]とされていたが近年の調査研究により、約1億年前の[[白亜紀]]後期の[[火山活動]]により[[カルデラ]]湖底に噴出した[[マグマ]]が湖水によって急速に冷却、破砕されてできた流紋岩質の[[水中自破砕溶岩]]（[[ハイアロクラスタイト]]）であると特定されている<ref>[https://www.gsj.jp/data/50KGM/PDF/GSJ_MAP_G050_12071_2003_D.pdf 高砂地域の地質 第12章 資源地質 2.採石及び砕石] [[地質調査総合センター]]</ref>。石材としては、加工に適した柔らかさに持つ反面、十分な強度と粘りを有し、耐火性にも優れる。色調は本来は淡緑色であるが酸化による色調変化があり、石材としては青、黃、赤系統の色がある。宝殿石とも呼ばれる。
採石場の近くを流れる[[法華山谷川]]（洗川）からの舟運で西日本各地に運ばれ、時代を問わず建築物や[[石仏]]等に利用された。古墳時代中後期の[[大仙陵古墳|大仙陵古墳（仁徳天皇陵）]][[石棺]]、[[長持形石棺]]・[[家形石棺]]、[[奈良時代]]の[[平城宮]]および[[恭仁京|恭仁宮]]の[[礎石]]、[[飛鳥大仏]]の台座、[[姫路城]]の多くの[[石垣]]、近現代では[[皇居]][[吹上御苑]][[御所]]、[[国会議事堂]]のほか、[[旧造幣寮鋳造所正面玄関|旧造幣寮貨幣鋳造所]]、[[三井住友銀行大阪本店ビル]]（旧 住友ビルディング）、[[名古屋市公会堂]]の外壁が知られる<ref name="shitei" />。道標の石材として使用された事例も知られる<ref>井原卓也『東播磨の道標をたずねて』神戸新聞総合出版センター（2004年4月）ISBN 4-343-00267-5、146ページ</ref>。また一般住宅の塀や門柱に使用されている。墓石や石碑としても硬い[[御影石]]の加工が容易になるまで多用されていた。
文化財修復用の石材としても利用される。姫路城の石垣、鳥取藩主[[池田氏|池田家]]墓所の[[玉垣]]、[[山本能楽堂]]などが知られる<ref>[http://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/shakai/201501/0007703615.shtml 文化財修復で脚光 1700年の歴史持つ高砂・竜山石] [[神戸新聞]]{{リンク切れ|date=2020年7月}}</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} <references />
== 関連項目 == * [[竜山石]] * [[石の宝殿]]
== 外部リンク == * [http://www.city.takasago.lg.jp/index.cfm/18,36295,175,html 国史跡「石の宝殿及び竜山石採石遺跡」] 高砂市 * [https://kotobank.jp/word/竜山石-321057 竜山石] コトバンク

revision.text:
[[File:Tatsuyama stone.jpg|thumb|Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins]] '''Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins''' (竜山石採石遺跡, Tatsuyamaishi Saiseki Iseki) are archaeological remains located in {{ill|Takasago, Hyōgo|ja|高砂市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}, Japan. Together with the nearby {{ill|Ishi no Hōden#Aokigami Shrine's Ishi no Hōden|ja|石の宝殿}}, situated about 200 meters away, the site has been designated as a {{ill|Historic Site (Japan)|ja|史跡}} by the Japanese government.<ref name="文化遺産オンライン" />
== Overview == The Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins span a total area of 670,000 square meters, with traces of quarrying observed across 162 locations. These sites document the production of {{ill|Tatsuyama Stone|ja|竜山石}}, a type of stone material quarried in Takasago City for approximately 1,700 years, from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} to the present day.<ref name="shitei">[http://www.hyogo-c.ed.jp/~board-bo/kisya26/2606/2606195.pdf Regarding the Designation of Historic Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments (National Historic Sites)] Hyōgo Prefecture Board of Education {{dead link|date=July 2020}}</ref> On {{ill|October 6, 2014|ja|2014年10月6日}}, an 110,000-square-meter section spanning 31 sites across {{ill|Mount Tatsuyama (Hyōgo)|ja|竜山}} and {{ill|Mount Hōden|ja|宝殿山}} was designated a National Historic Site as part of the "Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins" group.<ref name="文化遺産オンライン">{{文化遺産オンライン|210377|Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins}}</ref> This is the first ancient stone quarry to receive such a designation.<ref name="shitei" /><ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20140621-DL7M3INHVFINPOAXAFCBCSR2PE/ National Historic Site Designation for Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins Recommended to the Minister of Education] {{ill|Sankei Shimbun|ja|産経新聞}}</ref>
=== Tatsuyama Stone === Tatsuyama Stone, quarried from the {{ill|Hōden geological layer|ja|宝殿層}} on the right bank of the {{ill|Kakogawa River|ja|加古川}}, was traditionally classified as a type of tuffaceous rhyolitic welded tuff. Recent studies have identified it as rhyolitic {{ill|hyaloclastite|ja|ハイアロクラスタイト}}, formed approximately 100 million years ago during the late {{ill|Cretaceous|ja|白亜紀}}. Volcanic activity generated magma that erupted into a caldera lake, rapidly cooling and fragmenting upon contact with the water.<ref>[https://www.gsj.jp/data/50KGM/PDF/GSJ_MAP_G050_12071_2003_D.pdf Geology of the Takasago Area: Chapter 12, Resource Geology, Section 2: Quarrying and Crushed Stone] {{ill|Geological Survey of Japan|ja|地質調査総合センター}}</ref> The stone is easy to process due to its softness, while maintaining strength, resilience, and excellent fire resistance. Its original pale green color often changes over time due to oxidation, with shades of blue, yellow, and red also common. It is also referred to as Hōden Stone.
Tatsuyama Stone has been transported via the {{ill|Hokkesan Tanigawa River|ja|法華山谷川}} (Araikawa River) to various parts of western Japan. It has been widely used in construction, including ancient structures such as the {{ill|stone coffin|ja|石棺}} of the {{ill|Daisenryō Kofun|ja|大仙陵古墳}} (burial site of Emperor Nintoku), long box-shaped stone coffins, and house-shaped stone coffins from the mid-Kofun period. Other uses include foundation stones of the {{ill|Heijō Palace|ja|平城宮}} and {{ill|Kuni Palace|ja|恭仁宮}} during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, the pedestal of the {{ill|Asuka Great Buddha|ja|飛鳥大仏}}, stone walls of {{ill|Himeji Castle|ja|姫路城}}, and more modern structures such as the {{ill|Tokyo Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}} grounds, the {{ill|National Diet Building|ja|国会議事堂}}, and architectural landmarks like the {{ill|Former Coinage Bureau Building|ja|旧造幣寮鋳造所正面玄関}}, {{ill|Sumitomo Bank Osaka Head Office Building|ja|三井住友銀行大阪本店ビル}}, and {{ill|Nagoya City Public Hall|ja|名古屋市公会堂}}.<ref name="shitei" /> Tatsuyama Stone has also been used for waymarkers, residential walls, gates, tombstones, and monuments, and remains a choice material for cultural property restoration, including the stone walls of Himeji Castle and other notable sites.<ref>[http://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/shakai/201501/0007703615.shtml Tatsuyama Stone: Reviving a 1,700-Year Quarrying Legacy for Cultural Heritage Restoration] {{ill|Kobe Shimbun|ja|神戸新聞}}{{dead link|date=July 2020}}</ref>

[[File:Tatsuyama stone.jpg|thumb|Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins]] '''Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins''' (竜山石採石遺跡, Tatsuyamaishi Saiseki Iseki) are archaeological remains located in {{ill|Takasago, Hyōgo|ja|高砂市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}, Japan. Together with the nearby {{ill|Ishi no Hōden#Aokigami Shrine's Ishi no Hōden|ja|石の宝殿}}, situated about 200 meters away, the site has been designated as a {{ill|Historic Site (Japan)|ja|史跡}} by the Japanese government.<ref name="文化遺産オンライン" />
== Overview == The Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins span a total area of 670,000 square meters, with traces of quarrying observed across 162 locations. These sites document the production of {{ill|Tatsuyama Stone|ja|竜山石}}, a type of stone material quarried in Takasago City for approximately 1,700 years, from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} to the present day.<ref name="shitei">[http://www.hyogo-c.ed.jp/~board-bo/kisya26/2606/2606195.pdf Regarding the Designation of Historic Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments (National Historic Sites)] Hyōgo Prefecture Board of Education {{dead link|date=July 2020}}</ref> On {{ill|October 6, 2014|ja|2014年10月6日}}, an 110,000-square-meter section spanning 31 sites across {{ill|Mount Tatsuyama (Hyōgo)|ja|竜山}} and {{ill|Mount Hōden|ja|宝殿山}} was designated a National Historic Site as part of the "Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins" group.<ref name="文化遺産オンライン">{{文化遺産オンライン|210377|Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins}}</ref> This is the first ancient stone quarry to receive such a designation.<ref name="shitei" /><ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20140621-DL7M3INHVFINPOAXAFCBCSR2PE/ National Historic Site Designation for Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins Recommended to the Minister of Education] {{ill|Sankei Shimbun|ja|産経新聞}}</ref>
=== Tatsuyama Stone === Tatsuyama Stone, quarried from the {{ill|Hōden geological layer|ja|宝殿層}} on the right bank of the {{ill|Kakogawa River|ja|加古川}}, was traditionally classified as a type of tuffaceous rhyolitic welded tuff. Recent studies have identified it as rhyolitic {{ill|hyaloclastite|ja|ハイアロクラスタイト}}, formed approximately 100 million years ago during the late {{ill|Cretaceous|ja|白亜紀}}. Volcanic activity generated magma that erupted into a caldera lake, rapidly cooling and fragmenting upon contact with the water.<ref>[https://www.gsj.jp/data/50KGM/PDF/GSJ_MAP_G050_12071_2003_D.pdf Geology of the Takasago Area: Chapter 12, Resource Geology, Section 2: Quarrying and Crushed Stone] {{ill|Geological Survey of Japan|ja|地質調査総合センター}}</ref> The stone is easy to process due to its softness, while maintaining strength, resilience, and excellent fire resistance. Its original pale green color often changes over time due to oxidation, with shades of blue, yellow, and red also common. It is also referred to as Hōden Stone.
Tatsuyama Stone has been transported via the {{ill|Hokkesan Tanigawa River|ja|法華山谷川}} (Araikawa River) to various parts of western Japan. It has been widely used in construction, including ancient structures such as the {{ill|stone coffin|ja|石棺}} of the {{ill|Daisenryō Kofun|ja|大仙陵古墳}} (burial site of Emperor Nintoku), long box-shaped stone coffins, and house-shaped stone coffins from the mid-Kofun period. Other uses include foundation stones of the {{ill|Heijō Palace|ja|平城宮}} and {{ill|Kuni Palace|ja|恭仁宮}} during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, the pedestal of the {{ill|Asuka Great Buddha|ja|飛鳥大仏}}, stone walls of {{ill|Himeji Castle|ja|姫路城}}, and more modern structures such as the {{ill|Tokyo Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}} grounds, the {{ill|National Diet Building|ja|国会議事堂}}, and architectural landmarks like the {{ill|Former Coinage Bureau Building|ja|旧造幣寮鋳造所正面玄関}}, {{ill|Sumitomo Bank Osaka Head Office Building|ja|三井住友銀行大阪本店ビル}}, and {{ill|Nagoya City Public Hall|ja|名古屋市公会堂}}.<ref name="shitei" /> Tatsuyama Stone has also been used for waymarkers, residential walls, gates, tombstones, and monuments, and remains a choice material for cultural property restoration, including the stone walls of Himeji Castle and other notable sites.<ref>[http://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/shakai/201501/0007703615.shtml Tatsuyama Stone: Reviving a 1,700-Year Quarrying Legacy for Cultural Heritage Restoration] {{ill|Kobe Shimbun|ja|神戸新聞}}{{dead link|date=July 2020}}</ref>

[[File:Tatsuyama stone.jpg|thumb|Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins]] '''Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins''' (竜山石採石遺跡, Tatsuyamaishi Saiseki Iseki) are archaeological remains located in {{ill|Takasago, Hyōgo|ja|高砂市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}, Japan. Together with the nearby {{ill|Ishi no Hōden#Aokigami Shrine's Ishi no Hōden|ja|石の宝殿}}, situated about 200 meters away, the site has been designated as a {{ill|Historic Site (Japan)|ja|史跡}} by the Japanese government.<ref name="文化遺産オンライン" />
== Overview == The Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins span a total area of 670,000 square meters, with traces of quarrying observed across 162 locations. These sites document the production of {{ill|Tatsuyama Stone|ja|竜山石}}, a type of stone material quarried in Takasago City for approximately 1,700 years, from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} to the present day.<ref name="shitei">[http://www.hyogo-c.ed.jp/~board-bo/kisya26/2606/2606195.pdf Regarding the Designation of Historic Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments (National Historic Sites)] Hyōgo Prefecture Board of Education {{dead link|date=July 2020}}</ref> On {{ill|October 6, 2014|ja|2014年10月6日}}, an 110,000-square-meter section spanning 31 sites across {{ill|Mount Tatsuyama (Hyōgo)|ja|竜山}} and {{ill|Mount Hōden|ja|宝殿山}} was designated a National Historic Site as part of the "Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins" group.<ref name="文化遺産オンライン">{{文化遺産オンライン|210377|Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins}}</ref> This is the first ancient stone quarry to receive such a designation.<ref name="shitei" /><ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20140621-DL7M3INHVFINPOAXAFCBCSR2PE/ National Historic Site Designation for Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins Recommended to the Minister of Education] {{ill|Sankei Shimbun|ja|産経新聞}}</ref>
=== Tatsuyama Stone === Tatsuyama Stone, quarried from the {{ill|Hōden geological layer|ja|宝殿層}} on the right bank of the {{ill|Kakogawa River|ja|加古川}}, was traditionally classified as a type of tuffaceous rhyolitic welded tuff. Recent studies have identified it as rhyolitic {{ill|hyaloclastite|ja|ハイアロクラスタイト}}, formed approximately 100 million years ago during the late {{ill|Cretaceous|ja|白亜紀}}. Volcanic activity generated magma that erupted into a caldera lake, rapidly cooling and fragmenting upon contact with the water.<ref>[https://www.gsj.jp/data/50KGM/PDF/GSJ_MAP_G050_12071_2003_D.pdf Geology of the Takasago Area: Chapter 12, Resource Geology, Section 2: Quarrying and Crushed Stone] {{ill|Geological Survey of Japan|ja|地質調査総合センター}}</ref> The stone is easy to process due to its softness, while maintaining strength, resilience, and excellent fire resistance. Its original pale green color often changes over time due to oxidation, with shades of blue, yellow, and red also common. It is also referred to as Hōden Stone.
Tatsuyama Stone has been transported via the {{ill|Hokkesan Tanigawa River|ja|法華山谷川}} (Araikawa River) to various parts of western Japan. It has been widely used in construction, including ancient structures such as the {{ill|stone coffin|ja|石棺}} of the {{ill|Daisenryō Kofun|ja|大仙陵古墳}} (burial site of Emperor Nintoku), long box-shaped stone coffins, and house-shaped stone coffins from the mid-Kofun period. Other uses include foundation stones of the {{ill|Heijō Palace|ja|平城宮}} and {{ill|Kuni Palace|ja|恭仁宮}} during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, the pedestal of the {{ill|Asuka Great Buddha|ja|飛鳥大仏}}, stone walls of {{ill|Himeji Castle|ja|姫路城}}, and more modern structures such as the {{ill|Tokyo Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}} grounds, the {{ill|National Diet Building|ja|国会議事堂}}, and architectural landmarks like the {{ill|Former Coinage Bureau Building|ja|旧造幣寮鋳造所正面玄関}}, {{ill|Sumitomo Bank Osaka Head Office Building|ja|三井住友銀行大阪本店ビル}}, and {{ill|Nagoya City Public Hall|ja|名古屋市公会堂}}.<ref name="shitei" /> Tatsuyama Stone has also been used for waymarkers, residential walls, gates, tombstones, and monuments, and remains a choice material for cultural property restoration, including the stone walls of Himeji Castle and other notable sites.<ref>[http://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/shakai/201501/0007703615.shtml Tatsuyama Stone: Reviving a 1,700-Year Quarrying Legacy for Cultural Heritage Restoration] {{ill|Kobe Shimbun|ja|神戸新聞}}{{dead link|date=July 2020}}</ref>

{{Coord|34|46|51.0|N|134|47|41.0|E|region:JP-28|display=title}} {{Mountain-stub}} {{Architecture-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:たつやまいしさいせきいせき}} [[Category:History of Takasago]] [[Category:National Historic Sites of Japan in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Quarries]] [[Category:original pages]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Tatsuyama quarry}}[[category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages_with_redlinks_to_fill]][[Category:original pages]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Tatsuyama quarry}}[[category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Tatsuyama quarry}}[[category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Tatsuyama quarry}}[[category:original pages]] [[category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Tatsuyama quarry}}[[category:original pages]] [[category:original pages]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Tatsuyama quarry]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Tatsuyama quarry]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:newer pages]][[Category:newer pages]][[Category:newer pages]][[ja:竜山石採石遺跡]]

[[File:Tatsuyama stone.jpg|thumb|Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins]] '''Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins''' (竜山石採石遺跡, Tatsuyamaishi Saiseki Iseki) are archaeological remains located in {{ill|Takasago, Hyōgo|ja|高砂市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}, Japan. Together with the nearby {{ill|Ishi no Hōden#Aokigami Shrine's Ishi no Hōden|ja|石の宝殿}}, situated about 200 meters away, the site has been designated as a {{ill|Historic Site (Japan)|ja|史跡}} by the Japanese government.<ref name="文化遺産オンライン" />
== Overview == The Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins span a total area of 670,000 square meters, with traces of quarrying observed across 162 locations. These sites document the production of {{ill|Tatsuyama Stone|ja|竜山石}}, a type of stone material quarried in Takasago City for approximately 1,700 years, from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} to the present day.<ref name="shitei">[http://www.hyogo-c.ed.jp/~board-bo/kisya26/2606/2606195.pdf Regarding the Designation of Historic Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments (National Historic Sites)] Hyōgo Prefecture Board of Education {{dead link|date=July 2020}}</ref> On {{ill|October 6, 2014|ja|2014年10月6日}}, an 110,000-square-meter section spanning 31 sites across {{ill|Mount Tatsuyama (Hyōgo)|ja|竜山}} and {{ill|Mount Hōden|ja|宝殿山}} was designated a National Historic Site as part of the "Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins" group.<ref name="文化遺産オンライン">{{文化遺産オンライン|210377|Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins}}</ref> This is the first ancient stone quarry to receive such a designation.<ref name="shitei" /><ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20140621-DL7M3INHVFINPOAXAFCBCSR2PE/ National Historic Site Designation for Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins Recommended to the Minister of Education] {{ill|Sankei Shimbun|ja|産経新聞}}</ref>
=== Tatsuyama Stone === Tatsuyama Stone, quarried from the {{ill|Hōden geological layer|ja|宝殿層}} on the right bank of the {{ill|Kakogawa River|ja|加古川}}, was traditionally classified as a type of tuffaceous rhyolitic welded tuff. Recent studies have identified it as rhyolitic {{ill|hyaloclastite|ja|ハイアロクラスタイト}}, formed approximately 100 million years ago during the late {{ill|Cretaceous|ja|白亜紀}}. Volcanic activity generated magma that erupted into a caldera lake, rapidly cooling and fragmenting upon contact with the water.<ref>[https://www.gsj.jp/data/50KGM/PDF/GSJ_MAP_G050_12071_2003_D.pdf Geology of the Takasago Area: Chapter 12, Resource Geology, Section 2: Quarrying and Crushed Stone] {{ill|Geological Survey of Japan|ja|地質調査総合センター}}</ref> The stone is easy to process due to its softness, while maintaining strength, resilience, and excellent fire resistance. Its original pale green color often changes over time due to oxidation, with shades of blue, yellow, and red also common. It is also referred to as Hōden Stone.
Tatsuyama Stone has been transported via the {{ill|Hokkesan Tanigawa River|ja|法華山谷川}} (Araikawa River) to various parts of western Japan. It has been widely used in construction, including ancient structures such as the {{ill|stone coffin|ja|石棺}} of the {{ill|Daisenryō Kofun|ja|大仙陵古墳}} (burial site of Emperor Nintoku), long box-shaped stone coffins, and house-shaped stone coffins from the mid-Kofun period. Other uses include foundation stones of the {{ill|Heijō Palace|ja|平城宮}} and {{ill|Kuni Palace|ja|恭仁宮}} during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, the pedestal of the {{ill|Asuka Great Buddha|ja|飛鳥大仏}}, stone walls of {{ill|Himeji Castle|ja|姫路城}}, and more modern structures such as the {{ill|Tokyo Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}} grounds, the {{ill|National Diet Building|ja|国会議事堂}}, and architectural landmarks like the {{ill|Former Coinage Bureau Building|ja|旧造幣寮鋳造所正面玄関}}, {{ill|Sumitomo Bank Osaka Head Office Building|ja|三井住友銀行大阪本店ビル}}, and {{ill|Nagoya City Public Hall|ja|名古屋市公会堂}}.<ref name="shitei" /> Tatsuyama Stone has also been used for waymarkers, residential walls, gates, tombstones, and monuments, and remains a choice material for cultural property restoration, including the stone walls of Himeji Castle and other notable sites.<ref>[http://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/shakai/201501/0007703615.shtml Tatsuyama Stone: Reviving a 1,700-Year Quarrying Legacy for Cultural Heritage Restoration] {{ill|Kobe Shimbun|ja|神戸新聞}}{{dead link|date=July 2020}}</ref>

{{Coord|34|46|51.0|N|134|47|41.0|E|region:JP-28|display=title}} {{Mountain-stub}} {{Architecture-stub}} {{DEFAULTSORT:たつやまいしさいせきいせき}} [[Category:History of Takasago]] [[Category:National Historic Sites of Japan in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Quarries]] [[Category:original pages]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Tatsuyama quarry}}[[category:original pages]] [[Category:Pages_with_redlinks_to_fill]][[Category:original pages]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Tatsuyama quarry}}[[category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Tatsuyama quarry}}[[category:original pages]] [[Category:original pages]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Tatsuyama quarry}}[[category:original pages]] [[category:original pages]][[Category:original pages]]{{DEFAULTSORT:Tatsuyama quarry}}[[category:original pages]] [[category:original pages]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Tatsuyama quarry]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Tatsuyama quarry]][[Category:pp]] [[Category:original pages]][[category:Tatsuyama quarry]][[category:original pages]] [[Category:pp]][[category:Tatsuyama quarry]][[Category:pp]] [[ja:竜山石採石遺跡]]

[[File:Tatsuyama stone.jpg|thumb|Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins]] '''Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins''' (竜山石採石遺跡, Tatsuyamaishi Saiseki Iseki) are archaeological remains located in {{ill|Takasago, Hyōgo|ja|高砂市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}, Japan. Together with the nearby {{ill|Ishi no Hōden#Aokigami Shrine's Ishi no Hōden|ja|石の宝殿}}, situated about 200 meters away, the site has been designated as a {{ill|Historic Site (Japan)|ja|史跡}} by the Japanese government.<ref name="文化遺産オンライン" />
== Overview == The Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins span a total area of 670,000 square meters, with traces of quarrying observed across 162 locations. These sites document the production of {{ill|Tatsuyama Stone|ja|竜山石}}, a type of stone material quarried in Takasago City for approximately 1,700 years, from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} to the present day.<ref name="shitei">[http://www.hyogo-c.ed.jp/~board-bo/kisya26/2606/2606195.pdf Regarding the Designation of Historic Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments (National Historic Sites)] Hyōgo Prefecture Board of Education {{dead link|date=July 2020}}</ref> On {{ill|October 6, 2014|ja|2014年10月6日}}, an 110,000-square-meter section spanning 31 sites across {{ill|Mount Tatsuyama (Hyōgo)|ja|竜山}} and {{ill|Mount Hōden|ja|宝殿山}} was designated a National Historic Site as part of the "Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins" group.<ref name="文化遺産オンライン">{{文化遺産オンライン|210377|Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins}}</ref> This is the first ancient stone quarry to receive such a designation.<ref name="shitei" /><ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20140621-DL7M3INHVFINPOAXAFCBCSR2PE/ National Historic Site Designation for Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins Recommended to the Minister of Education] {{ill|Sankei Shimbun|ja|産経新聞}}</ref>
=== Tatsuyama Stone === Tatsuyama Stone, quarried from the {{ill|Hōden geological layer|ja|宝殿層}} on the right bank of the {{ill|Kakogawa River|ja|加古川}}, was traditionally classified as a type of tuffaceous rhyolitic welded tuff. Recent studies have identified it as rhyolitic {{ill|hyaloclastite|ja|ハイアロクラスタイト}}, formed approximately 100 million years ago during the late {{ill|Cretaceous|ja|白亜紀}}. Volcanic activity generated magma that erupted into a caldera lake, rapidly cooling and fragmenting upon contact with the water.<ref>[https://www.gsj.jp/data/50KGM/PDF/GSJ_MAP_G050_12071_2003_D.pdf Geology of the Takasago Area: Chapter 12, Resource Geology, Section 2: Quarrying and Crushed Stone] {{ill|Geological Survey of Japan|ja|地質調査総合センター}}</ref> The stone is easy to process due to its softness, while maintaining strength, resilience, and excellent fire resistance. Its original pale green color often changes over time due to oxidation, with shades of blue, yellow, and red also common. It is also referred to as Hōden Stone.
Tatsuyama Stone has been transported via the {{ill|Hokkesan Tanigawa River|ja|法華山谷川}} (Araikawa River) to various parts of western Japan. It has been widely used in construction, including ancient structures such as the {{ill|stone coffin|ja|石棺}} of the {{ill|Daisenryō Kofun|ja|大仙陵古墳}} (burial site of Emperor Nintoku), long box-shaped stone coffins, and house-shaped stone coffins from the mid-Kofun period. Other uses include foundation stones of the {{ill|Heijō Palace|ja|平城宮}} and {{ill|Kuni Palace|ja|恭仁宮}} during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, the pedestal of the {{ill|Asuka Great Buddha|ja|飛鳥大仏}}, stone walls of {{ill|Himeji Castle|ja|姫路城}}, and more modern structures such as the {{ill|Tokyo Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}} grounds, the {{ill|National Diet Building|ja|国会議事堂}}, and architectural landmarks like the {{ill|Former Coinage Bureau Building|ja|旧造幣寮鋳造所正面玄関}}, {{ill|Sumitomo Bank Osaka Head Office Building|ja|三井住友銀行大阪本店ビル}}, and {{ill|Nagoya City Public Hall|ja|名古屋市公会堂}}.<ref name="shitei" /> Tatsuyama Stone has also been used for waymarkers, residential walls, gates, tombstones, and monuments, and remains a choice material for cultural property restoration, including the stone walls of Himeji Castle and other notable sites.<ref>[http://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/shakai/201501/0007703615.shtml Tatsuyama Stone: Reviving a 1,700-Year Quarrying Legacy for Cultural Heritage Restoration] {{ill|Kobe Shimbun|ja|神戸新聞}}{{dead link|date=July 2020}}</ref>

[[File:Tatsuyama stone.jpg|thumb|Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins]] '''Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins''' (竜山石採石遺跡, Tatsuyamaishi Saiseki Iseki) are archaeological remains located in {{ill|Takasago, Hyōgo|ja|高砂市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}, Japan. Together with the nearby {{ill|Ishi no Hōden#Aokigami Shrine's Ishi no Hōden|ja|石の宝殿}}, situated about 200 meters away, the site has been designated as a {{ill|Historic Site (Japan)|ja|史跡}} by the Japanese government.<ref name="文化遺産オンライン" />
== Overview == The Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins span a total area of 670,000 square meters, with traces of quarrying observed across 162 locations. These sites document the production of {{ill|Tatsuyama Stone|ja|竜山石}}, a type of stone material quarried in Takasago City for approximately 1,700 years, from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} to the present day.<ref name="shitei">[http://www.hyogo-c.ed.jp/~board-bo/kisya26/2606/2606195.pdf Regarding the Designation of Historic Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments (National Historic Sites)] Hyōgo Prefecture Board of Education {{dead link|date=July 2020}}</ref> On {{ill|October 6, 2014|ja|2014年10月6日}}, an 110,000-square-meter section spanning 31 sites across {{ill|Mount Tatsuyama (Hyōgo)|ja|竜山}} and {{ill|Mount Hōden|ja|宝殿山}} was designated a National Historic Site as part of the "Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins" group.<ref name="文化遺産オンライン">{{文化遺産オンライン|210377|Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins}}</ref> This is the first ancient stone quarry to receive such a designation.<ref name="shitei" /><ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20140621-DL7M3INHVFINPOAXAFCBCSR2PE/ National Historic Site Designation for Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins Recommended to the Minister of Education] {{ill|Sankei Shimbun|ja|産経新聞}}</ref>
=== Tatsuyama Stone === Tatsuyama Stone, quarried from the {{ill|Hōden geological layer|ja|宝殿層}} on the right bank of the {{ill|Kako River (Japan)|lt=Kakogawa River|ja|加古川}}, was traditionally classified as a type of tuffaceous rhyolitic welded tuff. Recent studies have identified it as rhyolitic {{ill|hyaloclastite|ja|ハイアロクラスタイト}}, formed approximately 100 million years ago during the late {{ill|Cretaceous|ja|白亜紀}}. Volcanic activity generated magma that erupted into a caldera lake, rapidly cooling and fragmenting upon contact with the water.<ref>[https://www.gsj.jp/data/50KGM/PDF/GSJ_MAP_G050_12071_2003_D.pdf Geology of the Takasago Area: Chapter 12, Resource Geology, Section 2: Quarrying and Crushed Stone] {{ill|Geological Survey of Japan|ja|地質調査総合センター}}</ref> The stone is easy to process due to its softness, while maintaining strength, resilience, and excellent fire resistance. Its original pale green color often changes over time due to oxidation, with shades of blue, yellow, and red also common. It is also referred to as Hōden Stone.
Tatsuyama Stone has been transported via the {{ill|Hokkesan Tanigawa River|ja|法華山谷川}} (Araikawa River) to various parts of western Japan. It has been widely used in construction, including ancient structures such as the {{ill|stone coffin|ja|石棺}} of the {{ill|Daisenryō Kofun|ja|大仙陵古墳}} (burial site of Emperor Nintoku), long box-shaped stone coffins, and house-shaped stone coffins from the mid-Kofun period. Other uses include foundation stones of the {{ill|Heijō Palace|ja|平城宮}} and {{ill|Kuni Palace|ja|恭仁宮}} during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, the pedestal of the {{ill|Asuka Great Buddha|ja|飛鳥大仏}}, stone walls of {{ill|Himeji Castle|ja|姫路城}}, and more modern structures such as the {{ill|Tokyo Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}} grounds, the {{ill|National Diet Building|ja|国会議事堂}}, and architectural landmarks like the {{ill|Former Coinage Bureau Building|ja|旧造幣寮鋳造所正面玄関}}, {{ill|Sumitomo Bank Osaka Head Office Building|ja|三井住友銀行大阪本店ビル}}, and {{ill|Nagoya City Public Hall|ja|名古屋市公会堂}}.<ref name="shitei" /> Tatsuyama Stone has also been used for waymarkers, residential walls, gates, tombstones, and monuments, and remains a choice material for cultural property restoration, including the stone walls of Himeji Castle and other notable sites.<ref>[http://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/shakai/201501/0007703615.shtml Tatsuyama Stone: Reviving a 1,700-Year Quarrying Legacy for Cultural Heritage Restoration] {{ill|Kobe Shimbun|ja|神戸新聞}}{{dead link|date=July 2020}}</ref>

=== Tatsuyama Stone === Tatsuyama Stone, quarried from the {{ill|Hōden geological layer|ja|宝殿層}} on the right bank of the {{ill|Kako River (Japan)|lt=Kakogawa River|ja|加古川}}, was traditionally classified as a type of tuffaceous rhyolitic welded tuff. Recent studies have identified it as rhyolitic {{ill|hyaloclastite|ja|ハイアロクラスタイト}}, formed approximately 100 million years ago during the late {{ill|Cretaceous|ja|白亜紀}}. Volcanic activity generated magma that erupted into a caldera lake, rapidly cooling and fragmenting upon contact with the water.<ref>[https://www.gsj.jp/data/50KGM/PDF/GSJ_MAP_G050_12071_2003_D.pdf Geology of the Takasago Area: Chapter 12, Resource Geology, Section 2: Quarrying and Crushed Stone] {{ill|Geological Survey of Japan|ja|地質調査総合センター}}</ref> The stone is easy to process due to its softness, while maintaining strength, resilience, and excellent fire resistance. Its original pale green color often changes over time due to oxidation, with shades of blue, yellow, and red also common. It is also referred to as Hōden Stone.
Tatsuyama Stone has been transported via the {{ill|Hokkesan Tanigawa River|ja|法華山谷川}} (Araikawa River) to various parts of western Japan. It has been widely used in construction, including ancient structures such as the {{ill|stone coffin|ja|石棺}} of the {{ill|Daisenryō Kofun|ja|大仙陵古墳}} (burial site of Emperor Nintoku), long box-shaped stone coffins, and house-shaped stone coffins from the mid-Kofun period. Other uses include foundation stones of the {{ill|Heijō Palace|ja|平城宮}} and {{ill|Kuni Palace|ja|恭仁宮}} during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, the pedestal of the {{ill|Asuka Great Buddha|ja|飛鳥大仏}}, stone walls of {{ill|Himeji Castle|ja|姫路城}}, and more modern structures such as the {{ill|Tokyo Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}} grounds, the {{ill|National Diet Building|ja|国会議事堂}}, and architectural landmarks like the {{ill|Former Coinage Bureau Building|ja|旧造幣寮鋳造所正面玄関}}, {{ill|Sumitomo Bank Osaka Head Office Building|ja|三井住友銀行大阪本店ビル}}, and {{ill|Nagoya City Public Hall|ja|名古屋市公会堂}}.<ref name="shitei" /> Tatsuyama Stone has also been used for waymarkers, residential walls, gates, tombstones, and monuments, and remains a choice material for cultural property restoration, including the stone walls of Himeji Castle and other notable sites.<ref>[http://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/shakai/201501/0007703615.shtml Tatsuyama Stone: Reviving a 1,700-Year Quarrying Legacy for Cultural Heritage Restoration] {{ill|Kobe Shimbun|ja|神戸新聞}}{{dead link|date=July 2020}}</ref>

[[File:Tatsuyama stone.jpg|thumb|Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins]] '''Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins''' (竜山石採石遺跡, Tatsuyamaishi Saiseki Iseki) are archaeological remains located in {{ill|Takasago, Hyōgo|ja|高砂市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}, Japan. Together with the nearby {{ill|lt=Ishi no Hōden|Ishi no Hōden#Aokigami Shrine's Ishi no Hōden|ja|石の宝殿}}, situated about 200 meters away, the site has been designated as a {{ill|Historic Site (Japan)|ja|史跡}} by the Japanese government.<ref name="文化遺産オンライン" />
== Overview == The Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins span a total area of 670,000 square meters, with traces of quarrying observed across 162 locations. These sites document the production of {{ill|Tatsuyama Stone|ja|竜山石}}, a type of stone material quarried in Takasago City for approximately 1,700 years, from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} to the present day.<ref name="shitei">[http://www.hyogo-c.ed.jp/~board-bo/kisya26/2606/2606195.pdf Regarding the Designation of Historic Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments (National Historic Sites)] Hyōgo Prefecture Board of Education {{dead link|date=July 2020}}</ref> On {{ill|October 6, 2014|ja|2014年10月6日}}, an 110,000-square-meter section spanning 31 sites across {{ill|Mount Tatsuyama (Hyōgo)|ja|竜山}} and {{ill|Mount Hōden|ja|宝殿山}} was designated a National Historic Site as part of the "Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins" group.<ref name="文化遺産オンライン">{{文化遺産オンライン|210377|Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins}}</ref> This is the first ancient stone quarry to receive such a designation.<ref name="shitei" /><ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20140621-DL7M3INHVFINPOAXAFCBCSR2PE/ National Historic Site Designation for Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins Recommended to the Minister of Education] {{ill|Sankei Shimbun|ja|産経新聞}}</ref>

These sites document the production of {{ill|Tatsuyama Stone|ja|竜山石}}, a type of stone material quarried in Takasago City for approximately 1,700 years, from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} to the present day.<ref name="shitei">[http://www.hyogo-c.ed.jp/~board-bo/kisya26/2606/2606195.pdf Regarding the Designation of Historic Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments (National Historic Sites)] Hyōgo Prefecture Board of Education {{dead link|date=July 2020}}</ref> On {{ill|October 6, 2014|ja|2014年10月6日}}, an 110,000-square-meter section spanning 31 sites across {{ill|Mount Tatsuyama (Hyōgo)|ja|竜山}} and {{ill|Mount Hōden|ja|宝殿山}} was designated a National Historic Site as part of the "Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins" group.<ref name="文化遺産オンライン">{{文化遺産オンライン|210377|Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins}}</ref> This is the first ancient stone quarry to receive such a designation.<ref name="shitei" /><ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20140621-DL7M3INHVFINPOAXAFCBCSR2PE/ National Historic Site Designation for Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins Recommended to the Minister of Education] {{ill|Sankei Shimbun|ja|産経新聞}}</ref>
=== Tatsuyama Stone === Tatsuyama Stone, quarried from the {{ill|Hōden geological layer|ja|宝殿層}} on the right bank of the {{ill|Kako River (Japan)|lt=Kakogawa River|ja|加古川}}, was traditionally classified as a type of tuffaceous rhyolitic welded tuff. Recent studies have identified it as rhyolitic {{ill|hyaloclastite|ja|ハイアロクラスタイト}}, formed approximately 100 million years ago during the late {{ill|Cretaceous|ja|白亜紀}}. Volcanic activity generated magma that erupted into a caldera lake, rapidly cooling and fragmenting upon contact with the water.<ref>[https://www.gsj.jp/data/50KGM/PDF/GSJ_MAP_G050_12071_2003_D.pdf Geology of the Takasago Area: Chapter 12, Resource Geology, Section 2: Quarrying and Crushed Stone] {{ill|Geological Survey of Japan|ja|地質調査総合センター}}</ref> The stone is easy to process due to its softness, while maintaining strength, resilience, and excellent fire resistance. Its original pale green color often changes over time due to oxidation, with shades of blue, yellow, and red also common. It is also referred to as Hōden Stone.
Tatsuyama Stone has been transported via the {{ill|Hokkesan Tanigawa River|ja|法華山谷川}} (Araikawa River) to various parts of western Japan. It has been widely used in construction, including ancient structures such as the {{ill|stone coffin|ja|石棺}} of the {{ill|Daisenryō Kofun|ja|大仙陵古墳}} (burial site of Emperor Nintoku), long box-shaped stone coffins, and house-shaped stone coffins from the mid-Kofun period. Other uses include foundation stones of the {{ill|Heijō Palace|ja|平城宮}} and {{ill|Kuni Palace|ja|恭仁宮}} during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, the pedestal of the {{ill|Asuka Great Buddha|ja|飛鳥大仏}}, stone walls of {{ill|Himeji Castle|ja|姫路城}}, and more modern structures such as the {{ill|Tokyo Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}} grounds, the {{ill|National Diet Building|ja|国会議事堂}}, and architectural landmarks like the {{ill|Former Coinage Bureau Building|ja|旧造幣寮鋳造所正面玄関}}, {{ill|Sumitomo Bank Osaka Head Office Building|ja|三井住友銀行大阪本店ビル}}, and {{ill|Nagoya City Public Hall|ja|名古屋市公会堂}}.<ref name="shitei" /> Tatsuyama Stone has also been used for waymarkers, residential walls, gates, tombstones, and monuments, and remains a choice material for cultural property restoration, including the stone walls of Himeji Castle and other notable sites.<ref>[http://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/shakai/201501/0007703615.shtml Tatsuyama Stone: Reviving a 1,700-Year Quarrying Legacy for Cultural Heritage Restoration] {{ill|Kobe Shimbun|ja|神戸新聞}}{{dead link|date=July 2020}}</ref>

Together with the nearby {{ill|lt=Ishi no Hōden|Ishi no Hōden#Aokigami Shrine's Ishi no Hōden|ja|石の宝殿}}, situated about 200 meters away, the site has been designated as a {{ill|Historic Site (Japan)|ja|史跡}} by the Japanese government.<ref name="文化遺産オンライン" />

[[File:Tatsuyama stone.jpg|thumb|Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins]] '''Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins''' (竜山石採石遺跡, Tatsuyamaishi Saiseki Iseki) are archaeological remains located in {{ill|Takasago, Hyōgo|ja|高砂市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}, Japan. Together with the nearby {{ill|lt=Ishi no Hōden|Ishi no Hōden#Aokigami Shrine's Ishi no Hōden|ja|石の宝殿}}, situated about 200 meters away, the site has been designated as a {{ill|Historic Site (Japan)|ja|史跡}} by the Japanese government.<ref name="文化遺産オンライン" />
== Overview == The Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins span a total area of 670,000 square meters, with traces of quarrying observed across 162 locations. These sites document the production of {{ill|Tatsuyama Stone|ja|竜山石}}, a type of stone material quarried in Takasago City for approximately 1,700 years, from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} to the present day.<ref name="shitei">[http://www.hyogo-c.ed.jp/~board-bo/kisya26/2606/2606195.pdf Regarding the Designation of Historic Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments (National Historic Sites)] Hyōgo Prefecture Board of Education {{dead link|date=July 2020}}</ref> On {{ill|October 6, 2014|ja|2014年10月6日}}, an 110,000-square-meter section spanning 31 sites across {{ill|Mount Tatsuyama (Hyōgo)|ja|竜山}} and {{ill|Mount Hōden|ja|宝殿山}} was designated a National Historic Site as part of the "Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins" group.<ref name="文化遺産オンライン">{{文化遺産オンライン|210377|Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins}}</ref> This is the first ancient stone quarry to receive such a designation.<ref name="shitei" /><ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20140621-DL7M3INHVFINPOAXAFCBCSR2PE/ National Historic Site Designation for Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins Recommended to the Minister of Education] {{ill|Sankei Shimbun|ja|産経新聞}}</ref>
=== Tatsuyama Stone === Tatsuyama Stone, quarried from the {{ill|Hōden geological layer|ja|宝殿層}} on the right bank of the {{ill|Kako River (Japan)|lt=Kakogawa River|ja|加古川}}, was traditionally classified as a type of tuffaceous rhyolitic welded tuff. Recent studies have identified it as rhyolitic {{ill|hyaloclastite|ja|ハイアロクラスタイト}}, formed approximately 100 million years ago during the late {{ill|Cretaceous|ja|白亜紀}}. Volcanic activity generated magma that erupted into a caldera lake, rapidly cooling and fragmenting upon contact with the water.<ref>[https://www.gsj.jp/data/50KGM/PDF/GSJ_MAP_G050_12071_2003_D.pdf Geology of the Takasago Area: Chapter 12, Resource Geology, Section 2: Quarrying and Crushed Stone] {{ill|Geological Survey of Japan|ja|地質調査総合センター}}</ref> The stone is easy to process due to its softness, while maintaining strength, resilience, and excellent fire resistance. Its original pale green color often changes over time due to oxidation, with shades of blue, yellow, and red also common. It is also referred to as Hōden Stone.
Tatsuyama Stone has been transported via the {{ill|Hokkesan Tanigawa River|ja|法華山谷川}} (Araikawa River) to various parts of western Japan. It has been widely used in construction, including ancient structures such as the {{ill|stone coffin|ja|石棺}} of the {{ill|Daisenryō Kofun|ja|大仙陵古墳}} (burial site of Emperor Nintoku), long box-shaped stone coffins, and house-shaped stone coffins from the mid-Kofun period. Other uses include foundation stones of the {{ill|Heijō Palace|ja|平城宮}} and {{ill|Kuni Palace|ja|恭仁宮}} during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, the pedestal of the {{ill|Asuka Great Buddha|ja|飛鳥大仏}}, stone walls of {{ill|Himeji Castle|ja|姫路城}}, and more modern structures such as the {{ill|Tokyo Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}} grounds, the {{ill|National Diet Building|ja|国会議事堂}}, and architectural landmarks like the {{ill|Former Coinage Bureau Building|ja|旧造幣寮鋳造所正面玄関}}, {{ill|Sumitomo Bank Osaka Head Office Building|ja|三井住友銀行大阪本店ビル}}, and {{ill|Nagoya City Public Hall|ja|名古屋市公会堂}}.<ref name="shitei" /> Tatsuyama Stone has also been used for waymarkers, residential walls, gates, tombstones, and monuments, and remains a choice material for cultural property restoration, including the stone walls of Himeji Castle and other notable sites.<ref>[http://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/shakai/201501/0007703615.shtml Tatsuyama Stone: Reviving a 1,700-Year Quarrying Legacy for Cultural Heritage Restoration] {{ill|Kobe Shimbun|ja|神戸新聞}}{{dead link|date=July 2020}}</ref>

[[File:Tatsuyama stone.jpg|thumb|Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins]] '''Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins''' (竜山石採石遺跡, Tatsuyamaishi Saiseki Iseki) are archaeological remains located in {{ill|Takasago, Hyōgo|ja|高砂市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}, Japan. Together with the nearby {{ill|lt=Ishi no Hōden|Ishi no Hōden#Aokigami Shrine's Ishi no Hōden|ja|石の宝殿}}, situated about 200 meters away, the site has been designated as a {{ill|Historic Site (Japan)|ja|史跡}} by the Japanese government.<ref name="文化遺産オンライン" />

[[File:Tatsuyama stone.jpg|thumb|Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins]] '''Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins''' (竜山石採石遺跡, Tatsuyamaishi Saiseki Iseki) are archaeological remains located in {{ill|Takasago, Hyōgo|ja|高砂市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}, Japan. Together with the nearby {{ill|lt=Ishi no Hōden|Ishi no Hōden#Aokigami Shrine's Ishi no Hōden|ja|石の宝殿}}, situated about 200 meters away, the site has been designated as a {{ill|Historic Site (Japan)|ja|史跡}} by the Japanese government.<ref name="文化遺産オンライン" />
== Overview == The Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins span a total area of 670,000 square meters, with traces of quarrying observed across 162 locations. These sites document the production of {{ill|Tatsuyama Stone|ja|竜山石}}, a type of stone material quarried in Takasago City for approximately 1,700 years, from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} to the present day.<ref name="shitei">[http://www.hyogo-c.ed.jp/~board-bo/kisya26/2606/2606195.pdf Regarding the Designation of Historic Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments (National Historic Sites)] Hyōgo Prefecture Board of Education {{dead link|date=July 2020}}</ref> On {{ill|October 6, 2014|ja|2014年10月6日}}, an 110,000-square-meter section spanning 31 sites across {{ill|Mount Tatsuyama (Hyōgo)|ja|竜山}} and {{ill|Mount Hōden|ja|宝殿山}} was designated a National Historic Site as part of the "Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins" group.<ref name="文化遺産オンライン">{{文化遺産オンライン|210377|Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins}}</ref> This is the first ancient stone quarry to receive such a designation.<ref name="shitei" /><ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20140621-DL7M3INHVFINPOAXAFCBCSR2PE/ National Historic Site Designation for Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins Recommended to the Minister of Education] {{ill|Sankei Shimbun|ja|産経新聞}}</ref>
=== Tatsuyama Stone === Tatsuyama Stone, quarried from the {{ill|Hōden geological layer|ja|宝殿層}} on the right bank of the {{ill|Kako River (Japan)|lt=Kakogawa River|ja|加古川}}, was traditionally classified as a type of tuffaceous rhyolitic welded tuff. Recent studies have identified it as rhyolitic {{ill|hyaloclastite|ja|ハイアロクラスタイト}}, formed approximately 100 million years ago during the late {{ill|Cretaceous|ja|白亜紀}}. Volcanic activity generated magma that erupted into a caldera lake, rapidly cooling and fragmenting upon contact with the water.<ref>[https://www.gsj.jp/data/50KGM/PDF/GSJ_MAP_G050_12071_2003_D.pdf Geology of the Takasago Area: Chapter 12, Resource Geology, Section 2: Quarrying and Crushed Stone] {{ill|Geological Survey of Japan|ja|地質調査総合センター}}</ref> The stone is easy to process due to its softness, while maintaining strength, resilience, and excellent fire resistance. Its original pale green color often changes over time due to oxidation, with shades of blue, yellow, and red also common. It is also referred to as Hōden Stone.
Tatsuyama Stone has been transported via the {{ill|Hokkesan Tanigawa River|ja|法華山谷川}} (Araikawa River) to various parts of western Japan. It has been widely used in construction, including ancient structures such as the {{ill|stone coffin|ja|石棺}} of the {{ill|Daisenryō Kofun|ja|大仙陵古墳}} (burial site of Emperor Nintoku), long box-shaped stone coffins, and house-shaped stone coffins from the mid-Kofun period. Other uses include foundation stones of the {{ill|Heijō Palace|ja|平城宮}} and {{ill|Kuni Palace|ja|恭仁宮}} during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, the pedestal of the {{ill|Asuka-dera|ja|飛鳥大仏|qq=Asuka-dera|lt=Asuka Great Buddha}}, stone walls of {{ill|Himeji Castle|ja|姫路城}}, and more modern structures such as the {{ill|Tokyo Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}} grounds, the {{ill|National Diet Building|ja|国会議事堂}}, and architectural landmarks like the {{ill|Former Coinage Bureau Building|ja|旧造幣寮鋳造所正面玄関}}, {{ill|Sumitomo Bank Osaka Head Office Building|ja|三井住友銀行大阪本店ビル}}, and {{ill|Nagoya City Public Hall|ja|名古屋市公会堂}}.<ref name="shitei" /> Tatsuyama Stone has also been used for waymarkers, residential walls, gates, tombstones, and monuments, and remains a choice material for cultural property restoration, including the stone walls of Himeji Castle and other notable sites.<ref>[http://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/shakai/201501/0007703615.shtml Tatsuyama Stone: Reviving a 1,700-Year Quarrying Legacy for Cultural Heritage Restoration] {{ill|Kobe Shimbun|ja|神戸新聞}}{{dead link|date=July 2020}}</ref>

=== Tatsuyama Stone === Tatsuyama Stone, quarried from the {{ill|Hōden geological layer|ja|宝殿層}} on the right bank of the {{ill|Kako River (Japan)|lt=Kakogawa River|ja|加古川}}, was traditionally classified as a type of tuffaceous rhyolitic welded tuff. Recent studies have identified it as rhyolitic {{ill|hyaloclastite|ja|ハイアロクラスタイト}}, formed approximately 100 million years ago during the late {{ill|Cretaceous|ja|白亜紀}}. Volcanic activity generated magma that erupted into a caldera lake, rapidly cooling and fragmenting upon contact with the water.<ref>[https://www.gsj.jp/data/50KGM/PDF/GSJ_MAP_G050_12071_2003_D.pdf Geology of the Takasago Area: Chapter 12, Resource Geology, Section 2: Quarrying and Crushed Stone] {{ill|Geological Survey of Japan|ja|地質調査総合センター}}</ref> The stone is easy to process due to its softness, while maintaining strength, resilience, and excellent fire resistance. Its original pale green color often changes over time due to oxidation, with shades of blue, yellow, and red also common. It is also referred to as Hōden Stone.
Tatsuyama Stone has been transported via the {{ill|Hokkesan Tanigawa River|ja|法華山谷川}} (Araikawa River) to various parts of western Japan. It has been widely used in construction, including ancient structures such as the {{ill|stone coffin|ja|石棺}} of the {{ill|Daisenryō Kofun|ja|大仙陵古墳}} (burial site of Emperor Nintoku), long box-shaped stone coffins, and house-shaped stone coffins from the mid-Kofun period. Other uses include foundation stones of the {{ill|Heijō Palace|ja|平城宮}} and {{ill|Kuni Palace|ja|恭仁宮}} during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, the pedestal of the {{ill|Asuka-dera|ja|飛鳥大仏|qq=Asuka-dera|lt=Asuka Great Buddha}}, stone walls of {{ill|Himeji Castle|ja|姫路城}}, and more modern structures such as the {{ill|Tokyo Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}} grounds, the {{ill|National Diet Building|ja|国会議事堂}}, and architectural landmarks like the {{ill|Former Coinage Bureau Building|ja|旧造幣寮鋳造所正面玄関}}, {{ill|Sumitomo Bank Osaka Head Office Building|ja|三井住友銀行大阪本店ビル}}, and {{ill|Nagoya Civic Assembly Hall|ja|名古屋市公会堂}}.<ref name="shitei" /> Tatsuyama Stone has also been used for waymarkers, residential walls, gates, tombstones, and monuments, and remains a choice material for cultural property restoration, including the stone walls of Himeji Castle and other notable sites.<ref>[http://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/shakai/201501/0007703615.shtml Tatsuyama Stone: Reviving a 1,700-Year Quarrying Legacy for Cultural Heritage Restoration] {{ill|Kobe Shimbun|ja|神戸新聞}}{{dead link|date=July 2020}}</ref>

[[File:Tatsuyama stone.jpg|thumb|Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins]] '''Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins''' (竜山石採石遺跡, Tatsuyamaishi Saiseki Iseki) are archaeological remains located in {{ill|Takasago, Hyōgo|ja|高砂市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}, Japan. Together with the nearby {{ill|lt=Ishi no Hōden|Ishi no Hōden#Aokigami Shrine's Ishi no Hōden|ja|石の宝殿}}, situated about 200 meters away, the site has been designated as a {{ill|Historic Site (Japan)|ja|史跡}} by the Japanese government.<ref name="文化遺産オンライン" />
== Overview == The Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins span a total area of 670,000 square meters, with traces of quarrying observed across 162 locations. These sites document the production of {{ill|Tatsuyama Stone|ja|竜山石}}, a type of stone material quarried in Takasago City for approximately 1,700 years, from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} to the present day.<ref name="shitei">[http://www.hyogo-c.ed.jp/~board-bo/kisya26/2606/2606195.pdf Regarding the Designation of Historic Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments (National Historic Sites)] Hyōgo Prefecture Board of Education {{dead link|date=July 2020}}</ref> On {{ill|October 6, 2014|ja|2014年10月6日}}, an 110,000-square-meter section spanning 31 sites across {{ill|Mount Tatsuyama (Hyōgo)|ja|竜山}} and {{ill|Mount Hōden|ja|宝殿山}} was designated a National Historic Site as part of the "Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins" group.<ref name="文化遺産オンライン">{{文化遺産オンライン|210377|Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins}}</ref> This is the first ancient stone quarry to receive such a designation.<ref name="shitei" /><ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20140621-DL7M3INHVFINPOAXAFCBCSR2PE/ National Historic Site Designation for Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins Recommended to the Minister of Education] {{ill|Sankei Shimbun|ja|産経新聞}}</ref>
=== Tatsuyama Stone === Tatsuyama Stone, quarried from the {{ill|Hōden geological layer|ja|宝殿層}} on the right bank of the {{ill|Kako River (Japan)|lt=Kakogawa River|ja|加古川}}, was traditionally classified as a type of tuffaceous rhyolitic welded tuff. Recent studies have identified it as rhyolitic {{ill|hyaloclastite|ja|ハイアロクラスタイト}}, formed approximately 100 million years ago during the late {{ill|Cretaceous|ja|白亜紀}}. Volcanic activity generated magma that erupted into a caldera lake, rapidly cooling and fragmenting upon contact with the water.<ref>[https://www.gsj.jp/data/50KGM/PDF/GSJ_MAP_G050_12071_2003_D.pdf Geology of the Takasago Area: Chapter 12, Resource Geology, Section 2: Quarrying and Crushed Stone] {{ill|Geological Survey of Japan|ja|地質調査総合センター}}</ref> The stone is easy to process due to its softness, while maintaining strength, resilience, and excellent fire resistance. Its original pale green color often changes over time due to oxidation, with shades of blue, yellow, and red also common. It is also referred to as Hōden Stone.
Tatsuyama Stone has been transported via the {{ill|Hokkesan Tanigawa River|ja|法華山谷川}} (Araikawa River) to various parts of western Japan. It has been widely used in construction, including ancient structures such as the {{ill|stone coffin|ja|石棺}} of the {{ill|Daisenryō Kofun|ja|大仙陵古墳}} (burial site of Emperor Nintoku), long box-shaped stone coffins, and house-shaped stone coffins from the mid-Kofun period. Other uses include foundation stones of the {{ill|Heijō Palace|ja|平城宮}} and {{ill|Kuni Palace|ja|恭仁宮}} during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, the pedestal of the {{ill|Asuka-dera|ja|飛鳥大仏|qq=Asuka-dera|lt=Asuka Great Buddha}}, stone walls of {{ill|Himeji Castle|ja|姫路城}}, and more modern structures such as the {{ill|Tokyo Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}} grounds, the {{ill|National Diet Building|ja|国会議事堂}}, and architectural landmarks like the {{ill|Former Coinage Bureau Building|ja|旧造幣寮鋳造所正面玄関}}, {{ill|Sumitomo Bank Osaka Head Office Building|ja|三井住友銀行大阪本店ビル}}, and {{ill|Nagoya Civic Assembly Hall|ja|名古屋市公会堂}}.<ref name="shitei" /> Tatsuyama Stone has also been used for waymarkers, residential walls, gates, tombstones, and monuments, and remains a choice material for cultural property restoration, including the stone walls of Himeji Castle and other notable sites.<ref>[http://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/shakai/201501/0007703615.shtml Tatsuyama Stone: Reviving a 1,700-Year Quarrying Legacy for Cultural Heritage Restoration] {{ill|Kobe Shimbun|ja|神戸新聞}}{{dead link|date=July 2020}}</ref>

[[File:Tatsuyama stone.jpg|thumb|Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins]] '''Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins''' (竜山石採石遺跡, Tatsuyamaishi Saiseki Iseki) are archaeological remains located in {{ill|Takasago, Hyōgo|ja|高砂市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}, Japan. Together with the nearby {{ill|lt=Ishi no Hōden|Ishi no Hōden#Aokigami Shrine's Ishi no Hōden|ja|石の宝殿}}, situated about 200 meters away, the site has been designated as a {{ill|Historic Site (Japan)|ja|史跡}} by the Japanese government.<ref name="文化遺産オンライン" />
== Overview == The Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins span a total area of 670,000 square meters, with traces of quarrying observed across 162 locations. These sites document the production of {{ill|Tatsuyama Stone|ja|竜山石}}, a type of stone material quarried in Takasago City for approximately 1,700 years, from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} to the present day.<ref name="shitei">[http://www.hyogo-c.ed.jp/~board-bo/kisya26/2606/2606195.pdf Regarding the Designation of Historic Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments (National Historic Sites)] Hyōgo Prefecture Board of Education {{dead link|date=July 2020}}</ref> On {{ill|October 6, 2014|ja|2014年10月6日}}, an 110,000-square-meter section spanning 31 sites across {{ill|Mount Tatsuyama (Hyōgo)|ja|竜山}} and {{ill|Mount Hōden|ja|宝殿山}} was designated a National Historic Site as part of the "Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins" group.<ref name="文化遺産オンライン">{{文化遺産オンライン|210377|Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins}}</ref> This is the first ancient stone quarry to receive such a designation.<ref name="shitei" /><ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20140621-DL7M3INHVFINPOAXAFCBCSR2PE/ National Historic Site Designation for Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins Recommended to the Minister of Education] {{ill|Sankei Shimbun|ja|産経新聞}}</ref>
=== Tatsuyama Stone === Tatsuyama Stone, quarried from the {{ill|Hōden geological layer|ja|宝殿層}} on the right bank of the {{ill|Kako River (Japan)|lt=Kakogawa River|ja|加古川}}, was traditionally classified as a type of tuffaceous rhyolitic welded tuff. Recent studies have identified it as rhyolitic {{ill|hyaloclastite|ja|ハイアロクラスタイト}}, formed approximately 100 million years ago during the late {{ill|Cretaceous|ja|白亜紀}}. Volcanic activity generated magma that erupted into a caldera lake, rapidly cooling and fragmenting upon contact with the water.<ref>[https://www.gsj.jp/data/50KGM/PDF/GSJ_MAP_G050_12071_2003_D.pdf Geology of the Takasago Area: Chapter 12, Resource Geology, Section 2: Quarrying and Crushed Stone] {{ill|Geological Survey of Japan|ja|地質調査総合センター}}</ref> The stone is easy to process due to its softness, while maintaining strength, resilience, and excellent fire resistance. Its original pale green color often changes over time due to oxidation, with shades of blue, yellow, and red also common. It is also referred to as Hōden Stone.
Tatsuyama Stone has been transported via the {{ill|Hokkesan Tanigawa River|ja|法華山谷川}} (Araikawa River) to various parts of western Japan. It has been widely used in construction, including ancient structures such as the {{ill|stone coffin|ja|石棺}} of the {{ill|Daisenryō Kofun|ja|大仙陵古墳}} (burial site of Emperor Nintoku), long box-shaped stone coffins, and house-shaped stone coffins from the mid-Kofun period. Other uses include foundation stones of the {{ill|Heijō Palace|ja|平城宮}} and {{ill|Kuni-kyō|ja|恭仁宮|gg=Kuni-kyō|lt=Kuni Palace}} during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, the pedestal of the {{ill|Asuka-dera|ja|飛鳥大仏|qq=Asuka-dera|lt=Asuka Great Buddha}}, stone walls of {{ill|Himeji Castle|ja|姫路城}}, and more modern structures such as the {{ill|Tokyo Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}} grounds, the {{ill|National Diet Building|ja|国会議事堂}}, and architectural landmarks like the {{ill|Former Coinage Bureau Building|ja|旧造幣寮鋳造所正面玄関}}, {{ill|Sumitomo Bank Osaka Head Office Building|ja|三井住友銀行大阪本店ビル}}, and {{ill|Nagoya Civic Assembly Hall|ja|名古屋市公会堂}}.<ref name="shitei" /> Tatsuyama Stone has also been used for waymarkers, residential walls, gates, tombstones, and monuments, and remains a choice material for cultural property restoration, including the stone walls of Himeji Castle and other notable sites.<ref>[http://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/shakai/201501/0007703615.shtml Tatsuyama Stone: Reviving a 1,700-Year Quarrying Legacy for Cultural Heritage Restoration] {{ill|Kobe Shimbun|ja|神戸新聞}}{{dead link|date=July 2020}}</ref>

Other uses include foundation stones of the {{ill|Heijō Palace|ja|平城宮}} and {{ill|Kuni-kyō|ja|恭仁宮|gg=Kuni-kyō|lt=Kuni Palace}} during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, the pedestal of the {{ill|Asuka-dera|ja|飛鳥大仏|qq=Asuka-dera|lt=Asuka Great Buddha}}, stone walls of {{ill|Himeji Castle|ja|姫路城}}, and more modern structures such as the {{ill|Tokyo Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}} grounds, the {{ill|National Diet Building|ja|国会議事堂}}, and architectural landmarks like the {{ill|Former Coinage Bureau Building|ja|旧造幣寮鋳造所正面玄関}}, {{ill|Sumitomo Bank Osaka Head Office Building|ja|三井住友銀行大阪本店ビル}}, and {{ill|Nagoya Civic Assembly Hall|ja|名古屋市公会堂}}.<ref name="shitei" /> Tatsuyama Stone has also been used for waymarkers, residential walls, gates, tombstones, and monuments, and remains a choice material for cultural property restoration, including the stone walls of Himeji Castle and other notable sites.<ref>[http://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/shakai/201501/0007703615.shtml Tatsuyama Stone: Reviving a 1,700-Year Quarrying Legacy for Cultural Heritage Restoration] {{ill|Kobe Shimbun|ja|神戸新聞}}{{dead link|date=July 2020}}</ref>

revision.text:
[[File:Tatsuyama stone.jpg|thumb|Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins]] '''Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins''' (竜山石採石遺跡, Tatsuyamaishi Saiseki Iseki) are archaeological remains located in {{ill|Takasago, Hyōgo|ja|高砂市}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|ja|兵庫県}}, Japan. Together with the nearby {{ill|lt=Ishi no Hōden|Ishi no Hōden#Aokigami Shrine's Ishi no Hōden|ja|石の宝殿}}, situated about 200 meters away, the site has been designated as a {{ill|Historic Site (Japan)|ja|史跡}} by the Japanese government.<ref name="文化遺産オンライン" />
== Overview == The Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins span a total area of 670,000 square meters, with traces of quarrying observed across 162 locations. These sites document the production of {{ill|Tatsuyama Stone|ja|竜山石}}, a type of stone material quarried in Takasago City for approximately 1,700 years, from the {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} to the present day.<ref name="shitei">[http://www.hyogo-c.ed.jp/~board-bo/kisya26/2606/2606195.pdf Regarding the Designation of Historic Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments (National Historic Sites)] Hyōgo Prefecture Board of Education {{dead link|date=July 2020}}</ref> On {{ill|October 6, 2014|ja|2014年10月6日}}, an 110,000-square-meter section spanning 31 sites across {{ill|Mount Tatsuyama (Hyōgo)|ja|竜山}} and {{ill|Mount Hōden|ja|宝殿山}} was designated a National Historic Site as part of the "Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins" group.<ref name="文化遺産オンライン">{{文化遺産オンライン|210377|Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins}}</ref> This is the first ancient stone quarry to receive such a designation.<ref name="shitei" /><ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20140621-DL7M3INHVFINPOAXAFCBCSR2PE/ National Historic Site Designation for Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins Recommended to the Minister of Education] {{ill|Sankei Shimbun|ja|産経新聞}}</ref>
=== Tatsuyama Stone === Tatsuyama Stone, quarried from the {{ill|Hōden geological layer|ja|宝殿層}} on the right bank of the {{ill|Kako River (Japan)|lt=Kakogawa River|ja|加古川}}, was traditionally classified as a type of tuffaceous rhyolitic welded tuff. Recent studies have identified it as rhyolitic {{ill|hyaloclastite|ja|ハイアロクラスタイト}}, formed approximately 100 million years ago during the late {{ill|Cretaceous|ja|白亜紀}}. Volcanic activity generated magma that erupted into a caldera lake, rapidly cooling and fragmenting upon contact with the water.<ref>[https://www.gsj.jp/data/50KGM/PDF/GSJ_MAP_G050_12071_2003_D.pdf Geology of the Takasago Area: Chapter 12, Resource Geology, Section 2: Quarrying and Crushed Stone] {{ill|Geological Survey of Japan|ja|地質調査総合センター}}</ref> The stone is easy to process due to its softness, while maintaining strength, resilience, and excellent fire resistance. Its original pale green color often changes over time due to oxidation, with shades of blue, yellow, and red also common. It is also referred to as Hōden Stone.
Tatsuyama Stone has been transported via the {{ill|Hokkesan Tanigawa River|ja|法華山谷川}} (Araikawa River) to various parts of western Japan. It has been widely used in construction, including ancient structures such as the {{ill|stone coffin|ja|石棺}} of the {{ill|Daisenryō Kofun|ja|大仙陵古墳}} (burial site of Emperor Nintoku), long box-shaped stone coffins, and house-shaped stone coffins from the mid-Kofun period. Other uses include foundation stones of the {{ill|Heijō Palace|ja|平城宮}} and {{ill|Kuni-kyō|ja|恭仁宮|gg=Kuni-kyō|lt=Kuni Palace}} during the {{ill|Nara period|ja|奈良時代}}, the pedestal of the {{ill|Asuka-dera|ja|飛鳥大仏|qq=Asuka-dera|lt=Asuka Great Buddha}}, stone walls of {{ill|Himeji Castle|ja|姫路城}}, and more modern structures such as the {{ill|Tokyo Imperial Palace|ja|皇居}} grounds, the {{ill|National Diet Building|ja|国会議事堂}}, and architectural landmarks like the {{ill|Former Coinage Bureau Building|ja|旧造幣寮鋳造所正面玄関}}, {{ill|Sumitomo Bank Osaka Head Office Building|ja|三井住友銀行大阪本店ビル}}, and {{ill|Nagoya Civic Assembly Hall|ja|名古屋市公会堂}}.<ref name="shitei" /> Tatsuyama Stone has also been used for waymarkers, residential walls, gates, tombstones, and monuments, and remains a choice material for cultural property restoration, including the stone walls of Himeji Castle and other notable sites.<ref>[http://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/shakai/201501/0007703615.shtml Tatsuyama Stone: Reviving a 1,700-Year Quarrying Legacy for Cultural Heritage Restoration] {{ill|Kobe Shimbun|ja|神戸新聞}}{{dead link|date=July 2020}}</ref>
