[[File:Tatsuyama stone.jpg|thumb|Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins]] '''Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins''' (竜山石採石遺跡, Tatsuyamaishi Saiseki Iseki) are archaeological remains located in {{ill|Takasago, Hyōgo|en|Takasago, Hyōgo|WD=Q869037|lt=Takasago, Hyōgo|1=Takasago, Hyōgo}}, {{ill|Hyōgo Prefecture|en|Hyōgo Prefecture|WD=Q130290|lt=Hyōgo Prefecture|1=Hyōgo Prefecture}}, Japan. Together with the nearby {{ill|Ishi no Hōden|en|Ishi no Hōden|WD=Q11584736|lt=Ishi no Hōden|1=Ishi no Hōden}}, situated about 200 meters away, the site has been designated as a {{ill|Historic site|en|Historic site|WD=Q1081138|lt=Historic site of Japan|1=Historic site}}} by the Japanese government.<ref name="文化遺産オンライン" />
== Overview == The Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins span a total area of 670,000 square meters, with traces of quarrying observed across 162 locations. These sites document the production of {{ill|Tatsuyama Stone|ja|竜山石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatsuyama Stone|qq=}}, a type of stone material quarried in Takasago City for approximately 1,700 years, from the {{ill|Kofun period|en|Kofun period|WD=Q459939|lt=Kofun period|1=Kofun period}} to the present day.<ref name="shitei">[http://www.hyogo-c.ed.jp/~board-bo/kisya26/2606/2606195.pdf Regarding the Designation of Historic Sites, Scenic Spots, and Natural Monuments (National Historic Sites)] Hyōgo Prefecture Board of Education {{dead link|date=July 2020}}</ref> On {{ill|October 6, 2014|ja|2014th year 10月6日|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/October 6, 2014|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, an 110,000-square-meter section spanning 31 sites across {{ill|Longshan|en|Longshan|WD=Q421130|lt=Mount Tatsuyama (Hyōgo)|1=Longshan}} and {{ill|Mount Hōden|ja|宝殿山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mount Hōden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} was designated a National Historic Site as part of the "Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins" group.<ref name="文化遺産オンライン">{{文化遺産オンライン|210377|Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins}}</ref> This is the first ancient stone quarry to receive such a designation.<ref name="shitei" /><ref>[https://www.sankei.com/article/20140621-DL7M3INHVFINPOAXAFCBCSR2PE/ National Historic Site Designation for Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins Recommended to the Minister of Education] {{ill|Sankei Shimbun|en|Sankei Shimbun|WD=Q1197261|lt=Sankei Shimbun|1=Sankei Shimbun}}</ref>
=== Tatsuyama Stone === Tatsuyama Stone, quarried from the {{ill|Hōden geological layer|ja|宝殿層|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōden geological layer|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} on the right bank of the {{ill|Kako River (Japan)|en|Kako River (Japan)|WD=Q11398501|lt=Kakogawa River|1=Kako River (Japan)}}, was traditionally classified as a type of tuffaceous rhyolitic welded tuff. Recent studies have identified it as rhyolitic {{ill|Hyaloclastite|en|Hyaloclastite|WD=Q964204|lt=hyaloclastite|1=Hyaloclastite}}, formed approximately 100 million years ago during the late {{ill|Cretaceous|en|Cretaceous|WD=Q44626|lt=Cretaceous|1=Cretaceous}}. Volcanic activity generated magma that erupted into a caldera lake, rapidly cooling and fragmenting upon contact with the water.<ref>[https://www.gsj.jp/data/50KGM/PDF/GSJ_MAP_G050_12071_2003_D.pdf Geology of the Takasago Area: Chapter 12, Resource Geology, Section 2: Quarrying and Crushed Stone] {{ill|Geological Survey of Japan|en|Geological Survey of Japan|WD=Q11424703|lt=Geological Survey of Japan|1=Geological Survey of Japan}}</ref> The stone is easy to process due to its softness, while maintaining strength, resilience, and excellent fire resistance. Its original pale green color often changes over time due to oxidation, with shades of blue, yellow, and red also common. It is also referred to as Hōden Stone.
Tatsuyama Stone has been transported via the {{ill|Hokkesan Tanigawa River|ja|法華山谷川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hokkesan Tanigawa River|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Araikawa River) to various parts of western Japan. It has been widely used in construction, including ancient structures such as the {{ill|Sarcophagus|ja|石棺|1=Sekkan|1=Sekkan (Sarcophagus)|lt=Sarcophagus|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sarcophagus|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekkan|qq=}} of the {{ill|Daisenryō Kofun|ja|大仙陵古墳|1=Daisenryō Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisenryō Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Daisenryō Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (burial site of Emperor Nintoku), long box-type Sarcophaguss, and house-shaped Sarcophaguss from the mid-Kofun period. Other uses include foundation stones of the {{ill|Heijō Palace|en|Heijō Palace|WD=Q211585|lt=Heijō Palace|1=Heijō Palace}} and {{ill|Kuni-kyō|ja|恭仁宮|gg=Kuni-kyō|lt=Kuni Palace|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kuni-kyō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 恭仁京|1=Kuni-kyō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q1155335}} during the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|WD=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, the pedestal of the {{ill|Asuka-dera|ja|飛鳥大仏|11=Asuka-dera|lt=Asuka Great Buddha|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Asuka-dera|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 飛鳥寺|1=Asuka-dera|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q779755}}, stone walls of {{ill|Himeji Castle|en|Himeji Castle|WD=Q188754|lt=Himeji Castle|1=Himeji Castle}}, and more modern structures such as the {{ill|Tokyo Imperial Palace|en|Tokyo Imperial Palace|WD=Q500681|lt=Tokyo Imperial Palace|1=Tokyo Imperial Palace}} grounds, the {{ill|National Diet Building|en|National Diet Building|WD=Q1338856|lt=National Diet Building|1=National Diet Building}}, and architectural landmarks like the {{ill|Former Coinage Bureau Building|ja|旧造幣寮鋳造所正面玄関|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Former Coinage Bureau Building|qq=}}, {{ill|Sumitomo Bank Osaka Head Office Building|ja|三井住友銀行大阪本店ビル|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sumitomo Bank Osaka Head Office Building|qq=}}, and {{ill|Nagoya Civic Assembly Hall|en|Nagoya Civic Assembly Hall|WD=Q6958957|lt=Nagoya Civic Assembly Hall|1=Nagoya Civic Assembly Hall}}.<ref name="shitei" /> Tatsuyama Stone has also been used for waymarkers, residential walls, gates, tombstones, and monuments, and remains a choice material for cultural property restoration, including the stone walls of Himeji Castle and other notable sites.<ref>[http://www.kobe-np.co.jp/news/shakai/201501/0007703615.shtml Tatsuyama Stone: Reviving a 1,700-Year Quarrying Legacy for Cultural Heritage Restoration] {{ill|Kobe Shimbun|en|Kobe Shimbun|WD=Q13403521|lt=Kobe Shimbun|1=Kobe Shimbun}}{{dead link|date=July 2020}}</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Tatsuyama Stone|ja|竜山石|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatsuyama Stone|qq=}} * {{ill|Ishi no Hōden|en|Ishi no Hōden|WD=Q11584736|lt=Ishi no Hōden|1=Ishi no Hōden}}
== External Links == * [http://www.city.takasago.lg.jp/index.cfm/18,36295,175,html National Historic Site: Ishi no Hōden and Tatsuyama Stone Quarry Ruins] (Takasago City Official Site) * [https://kotobank.jp/word/竜山石-321057 Tatsuyama Stone] (Kotobank)
